This is the broken plural form showing multiple sins
The word 'sins' becomes 'our sins' by adding نَا (meaning 'our') at the end. This is what we are asking Allah to forgive.
Changed into a command telling someone to protect. The last letter drops off because it's a command.
Added نَا to show who is being protected (us). This connects to عَذَابَ (punishment) showing what we need protection from.
Added وَ at beginning to connect this request with the previous one (forgive us).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif after the second root letter turns the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of قِنَا (protect us from) in the ayah
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'fire' by adding vowels and changing the weak middle letter و to ا
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because this word follows عَذَابَ (punishment) in the phrase عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ (punishment of the fire)
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Adding an alif after the first letter and kasra after the second letter makes this word mean 'someone who has this quality'
Adding ين makes this refer to many people who are patient. This word is part of a list with وَٱلصَّادِقِينَ (and the truthful ones) and other praised qualities.
Adding ال makes it specific. The shadda on ص comes because it follows ال. The final fatḥa shows this is part of a list of qualities.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from the action to describing someone who does the action
Adding ال makes it specific and adds shadda to the first letter

steps.m: the truthful ones (object of implied praise)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it plural and shows it's being acted upon. This word is connected to الصابرين (the patient ones) in meaning.

steps.m: and the truthful ones (object of implied praise)
steps.n: Adding و connects this word to the previous word الصابرين

Adding an alif after the first letter makes this word mean 'someone who does the action'. This word is in a list of qualities being described, connected to the other words through the و (and) conjunction, all in the accusative case.
Adding vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Changes from singular (one person) to plural (many people)
Adding ال at the beginning makes it specific, like saying 'the' in English
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to the previous words in the list, and adding َ at the end shows this word is being acted upon
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show someone who does the action
Added ال to make it specific
Added ين to make it plural and show it's being acted upon. This word is part of a list of descriptions that are all objects, starting with الصابرين.

steps.m: and the ones who spend (plural object)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to the previous descriptions in the list

steps.m: to forgive or cover up faults

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make past tense 'he forgave'
Added مُسْتَ at the beginning to make it mean 'one who seeks/asks for' forgiveness, like turning 'forgave' into 'forgiveness seeker'
Added ال to make it specific
Added ين to make it plural (more than one person seeking forgiveness)

steps.m: and the ones who seek forgiveness
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect it with previous words in the list
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a basic noun
This is a plural form meaning multiple dawn times
Adding 'ال' makes the word specific
Adding preposition ب to indicate 'in/during'
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb. This word shows what Allah did - He witnessed/testified something, which is explained in the rest of the ayah.
Changed the root into the basic noun form meaning 'a deity'

This is a unique word transformation that happens only for the name of Allah. The definite article ال is added, the first letter is changed to ٱ, and the middle letter gets a shadda. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject of شَهِدَ (testified/witnessed).
Added shadda and fatha to show this is the emphasized form of the word meaning 'that'
Added هُ at the end to show this refers to what comes next in the ayah (that there is no god except Him)
Added a small و mark (ۥ) after هُ which is just a pronunciation helper and doesn't change the meaning
This is a negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'. In this ayah, it negates the word إِلٰهَ (deity/god) to say 'there is no deity'.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to be devoted to, to worship
deity, god (object of لَا - meaning 'there is no')
steps.n: Changed the first letter to إِ and added ا after ل to form the noun meaning 'god/deity'. This word is being negated by لَا in the ayah.
The small mark (ٰ) above shows the long 'aa' sound should be pronounced clearly

steps.m: deity, god (object of لَا - meaning 'there is no')
steps.n: The word gets a fatha mark at the end because it comes after لَا (meaning 'no/there is no')

steps.m: except (what comes after it from what came before)
steps.n: This is a special word that shows an exception - it tells us that what comes after it is different from what came before it. In this ayah, it's used to show that there is no god EXCEPT Allah, making a very strong statement about Allah being the only true god.

This is a pronoun that refers back to Allah (اللَّهُ) mentioned earlier in the ayah. In Arabic, هُوَ is used for masculine singular subjects, similar to 'he' or 'it' in English, but here it specifically refers to Allah.

steps.m: to have power or authority over something

steps.n: Changed the root 'to rule/have power' into a noun meaning 'angel' by adding the vowels a-a
Changes from single angel (مَلَك) to plural angels (مَلائِكَة) by adding ائِ in the middle and ة at the end - this is a special plural form
Added the definite article to specify these particular angels
Added conjunction to connect this with the previous statement
Added the nominative case ending to show this is a subject doing the witnessing (شَهِدَ)
This is a special word that means 'people who possess/have something'. In this ayah, it refers to people who possess knowledge (ٱلْعِلْمِ).
Added و with fatha (وَ) at the start to mean 'and', connecting this word to what came before it (وَٱلْمَلٰٓئِكَةُ - and the angels)
Added small circles (۟) above the و and ا to indicate proper pronunciation in Uthmani script. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changed from basic root meaning 'to know' into the noun 'knowledge' by adding the 'i' sound (kasra) after first letter
Added ال to make it specific knowledge rather than just any knowledge
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or is connected to أُو۟لُوا۟ (those who possess)
Changed و to ا and added ئ to show who does the action. This word describes Allah in this ayah.
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite
Changed from basic form to show action/state - added ًۢا at end to describe how Allah maintains justice (بِٱلْقِسْطِ)
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning the concept itself
Added ال to make it specific justice rather than any justice

steps.m: with/by justice (describing how قَآئِمًۢا/standing is done)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to show how something is done, and ِ at end because it follows the preposition بِ. This word explains how Allah, the angels, and people of knowledge are قَآئِمًۢا (standing/maintaining) - they do it with justice.
This is a basic negative particle that makes things not true - like saying 'not' or 'no' in English. Here it negates إِلٰهَ (deity) to say 'there is no deity'
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'la'
Changed the regular alif (ا) to an elongated alif (آ) to show that you stretch the sound when saying it
Changed first letter to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object after أَنَّهُۥ لَآ (that there is no). The alif above the ل (ٰ) is just a writing style variation.
This is a particle that shows an exception. In this ayah, it's excluding هُوَ (He/Allah) from the statement لَآ إِلٰهَ (there is no god), so it means 'there is no god except Him'
Added the long alif at the end to complete the word. This is a fixed part of this particle's spelling.

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a pronoun that refers back to Allah (ٱللَّهُ) mentioned earlier in the ayah. It's being used to emphasize that there is no deity except Him.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show a strong quality
Added the definite article to specify this is referring to Allah

steps.m: The Mighty One (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Added the 'u' mark at the end to show this is one of Allah's names in this ayah
Added vowels and ي to change from an action to describing someone who has wisdom

steps.m: The Wise One (a name/attribute of Allah)
steps.n: Adding ال makes this a specific title referring to Allah, showing this is one of His names

steps.m: The Wise One (attribute of Allah mentioned with ٱلْعَزِيزُ/The Mighty)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this word is paired with another attribute of Allah (ٱلْعَزِيزُ) in this ayah

This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. It's like adding 'definitely' at the start of a sentence. Here it emphasizes that ٱلدِّينَ (the religion) is definitely something.

steps.m: to judge, follow, or be accountable

steps.n: The root letters get a long 'ee' sound (kasra + ya) to make it mean the system/way you follow
The word gets 'the' (ال) at the start, and doubles the د sound with a shadda

steps.m: the religion/way of life (object of إِنَّ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed). Also, the initial alif is written as hamzat wasl (ٱ) because it's not pronounced when connected to previous words.
Adding kasra changes this from an action into a word showing location or relationship
Adding fatha shows this word connects to the word after it (اللَّهِ) to mean 'with/near Allah'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection. The alif at the start becomes a wasl form (ٱ).

steps.m: to submit, surrender, or be at peace

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha-kasra-fatha) to make 'he' past tense verb meaning submitted
Changed from 'he submitted' to the name of the action of submitting
Added ال (the) to make it mean 'the submission/Islam' instead of just 'submission'. The ُ ending shows it's completing the statement that starts with 'Indeed, the religion...'
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: and what/that which (connects to ٱخْتَلَفَ - differed)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this clause with the previous statement. This word introduces what differed (ٱخْتَلَفَ) among the People of the Book.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Adding ٱ and ت changes it to show people disagreeing with each other
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ٱلَّ makes it specific and definite

steps.m: those who (plural, refers to أُوتُوا۟ - those who were given)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. In this ayah, it specifically refers to those who were given the scripture.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he gave' to 'it was given' by adding damma (u sound) at start and kasra (i sound) in middle - this makes it passive, showing something received the action
Adds plural marker وا to show multiple people received the action. This word refers to the people who were given the Book (الْكِتٰبَ) mentioned right after it.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels and added ا to make it mean the result of writing (a book/writing)

steps.m: the book (object of أُوتُوا - were given)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the book') and َ to show it's receiving the action of being given
This is a basic exception word that shows contrast with what came before. In this ayah, it shows that the People of the Book only differed EXCEPT/AFTER knowledge came to them (not before).

steps.m: from (connects بَعْدِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word بَعْدِ (after) to what comes before it, showing when the disagreement happened.

steps.m: from (connects بَعْدِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: Added a small ن (called a 'noon ghunna') at the end to show how to pronounce it smoothly with the next word
Added vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to the root letters to make the word mean 'after' - works with مِنۢ before it
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because it follows مِنۢ (from/after) in the ayah
In this ayah, مَا is used as a negative particle that comes before the verb اخْتَلَفَ (differed/disagreed) to negate it. So together they mean 'did not differ/disagree'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The middle letter ي becomes ا because it's a weak letter. This word is connected to بَغْيًۢا (transgressing) in this ayah.
The extended alif (آ) adds emphasis to show this coming was significant or definitive
Added هُمُ which means 'them' (for a group of men). The ُ at the end shows this word connects to the next word.

steps.m: to know, understand, or be aware of

steps.n: Changed the verb idea into a noun by adding the vowel pattern ِ _ ْ. This makes it mean the thing itself (knowledge) instead of the action (to know).
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific (like 'the' in English) and ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: to wrongfully want more than one deserves
the act of wanting more than one deserves
steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun showing the action itself (like changing 'transgress' to 'transgression'). This word explains how/why the People of the Book disagreed (اخْتَلَفَ).

steps.m: out of wanting more than they deserved
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this describes how/why they differed - they differed because they wanted more than they deserved

steps.m: to be separate/clear from one another

steps.n: Changed the root meaning into a preposition that shows the space or relationship between things
Added هُمْ at the end to show this refers to 'them' (the People of the Book who were given the scripture, mentioned earlier in the verse)
This combines 'and' with a word meaning 'whoever' to connect a new condition
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he disbelieves (in بِـَٔايٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - the signs of Allah)
steps.n: The يَ at the start shows this is happening now or in the future. The sukun and damma show this is in a conditional statement - 'whoever disbelieves...'

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident

steps.n: Forms a noun meaning 'sign' from the basic concept of 'showing/making clear'
Adds a long alif at the start to emphasize the meaning
Makes the word plural to show multiple signs
Adds بِ to show these signs are being used as tools or means
The word gets a kasra (ِ) at the end because it belongs to Allah's name that follows it
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word
This is a particle that adds emphasis to what follows, like saying 'certainly' or 'indeed' in English
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This word connects to سَرِيعُ (swift) in the ayah, introducing Allah's attribute.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows the doubled ل sound.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَكْفُرْ - disbelieves in)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon by the verb يَكْفُرْ (disbelieves in). Also changed alif to wasl alif since it comes after another word.
Adds ي to show this is a quality or description. This word describes how Allah's reckoning is quick.
Added ُ at the end to show this is describing something
Added ِ on first letter and ا after second letter to turn the action into a noun meaning the process or result of counting
This word is owned by سَرِيعُ (swift), making the phrase mean 'swift of reckoning'
Adding definite article
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a condition - it shows that what comes after it depends on something else happening

steps.m: then if (connects this condition to what came before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition to the previous statement, showing sequence or result
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding alif shows the action is between two people arguing with each other
Changed ending to show many people arguing (like 'they' instead of 'he'), and added و to connect to next word
Added كَ at the end to show who they are arguing with (you)
The root letters ق-و-ل become قُلْ when making a command - just add ُ on ق and drop و to say 'say!'

steps.m: then say! (responding to حَآجُّوكَ - if they argue with you)
steps.n: Adds فَ to show this is what you should say in response to them arguing. This connects it to the previous phrase حَآجُّوكَ (they argue with you).
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Adding أَ at the start and changing the vowels makes the meaning stronger - showing complete submission
Adding تُ changes who did the action from 'he' to 'I', and adds a sukun before it
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the noun for 'face'
Added kasra to show this word connects to something after it
Added ى with fatha to show it means 'my face'. This is the object (thing being submitted) in the phrase 'I submitted my face'
This is Allah's proper name. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
Added لِ at the beginning to show the meaning 'to' or 'for'. The ا is removed when لِ is added.
Added kasra at the end to complete the proper grammatical form.
This is a connecting word that links to the verb that follows it (اتَّبَعَنِ meaning 'followed me'). The ِ at the end helps connect it smoothly to the next word.
Adding وَ at the start connects this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and doubled the ت to show complete or thorough following
Added نِ at the end to show the following was done to 'me'
Changed initial اِ to ٱ (this is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning)
The middle letter و is dropped and we add ُ on ق and ْ on ل to make it a command. This tells one person to do something.

steps.m: and say! (connected to فَقُلْ earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding وَ at the start connects this command to the previous command فَقُلْ in the ayah, showing these are related instructions
This is a special word used to point to someone or something specific. The ي shows it's talking about one person or thing.
Adding ين at the end makes it talk about many people instead of just one

steps.m: to those who (who are being addressed in أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتٰبَ)
steps.n: Adding لِ at the beginning means 'to'. The ل combines with ال to make a stronger ل sound (shown by ّ)
Connected the root letters together. The ى at the end is because this is a weak root (ends in ي).
Changed from 'he gave' to 'it was given' - the doer is not mentioned. Like saying 'I gave the book' vs 'the book was given'

steps.m: they were given (refers to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Changes from 'it was given' to 'they were given' by adding وا at the end to show multiple people
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
This pattern changes the verb into a noun for the thing that results from the action. In this ayah, كِتَاب refers to divine scripture.
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
The fatha at the end shows this word is the object (what was given) in the phrase 'those who were given the Book'

steps.m: unlettered person (one who remains in original state, not taught reading/writing)
steps.n: Adds special endings to the root 'mother/origin' to make a word meaning 'someone who stayed in their original state (unable to read/write)'
Makes the word plural to show we're talking about multiple unlettered people
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect to previous phrase

steps.m: and the specific unlettered people (referring to the Arabs who hadn't received scripture)
steps.n: Adds 'al' (ال) to make it refer to specific unlettered people rather than any unlettered people

steps.m: and the unlettered people (in accusative case)
steps.n: Adds the letter ن (with fatha) at the end to show this word is being acted upon
Adding أ at beginning shows active submission/surrender, especially used for submitting to Allah's will (becoming Muslim)
Adding تُ (tu) at the end changes it from 'he submitted' to 'I submitted' - like adding 'I' to show who did the action
Adding م after ت changes 'I submitted' to 'you all submitted' (plural past tense)

Adding ء with fatha at beginning turns statement into question (Did you submit?). This question appears in a context where the Prophet is instructed to ask the People of the Book if they have submitted to Allah.
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces the first part of an if-then statement. In this ayah, it starts the condition 'if they argue with you'

steps.m: and if/then if (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it, showing sequence or result
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the start changes the meaning from 'being peaceful' to 'making oneself peaceful by submitting to Allah' - it makes you do the action
Adding وا changes the subject from singular to plural masculine (they)
Added vowel marks to show this is a particle meaning 'certainly' or 'indeed'. The kasra (ِ) is added because another word follows it.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This connects to the word ٱهْتَدَوا۟ (they are guided) that comes after it.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a basic past tense verb. The ي at the end becomes ى because it's a weak letter at the end.

steps.m: he became guided (found the right path himself)
steps.n: The pattern shows someone receiving guidance themselves rather than guiding others

steps.m: they became guided (found the right path themselves)

steps.n: This is a compound particle combining 'and' (وَ) with 'if' (إِن)

steps.m: to turn away or withdraw from

steps.n: Changed root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels
Doubled middle letter to show complete/intensive turning away
Added تَ at beginning to show doing action to oneself
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added مَا at the end which changes the meaning from emphasis to restriction (only/just)
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
When adding a pronoun after عَلَى, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to connect with what comes next.
Added كَ at the end to show this affects 'you'. This كَ always means 'you' when added at the end of words.

steps.m: to deliver or convey a message

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed verb into noun meaning 'the act of delivering'
Added ال to make it specific
Changed the middle alif to a long alif, and added a u-sound at the end to show this word is doing the action
This is a unique proper name that doesn't follow normal word patterns. It's the personal name of God in Islam.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in بَصِيرٌۢ (All-Seeing)

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject of بَصِيرٌۢ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement
Added vowel marks (fatha-kasra) to make the root into a past tense verb meaning 'he saw'
Added ي to show this is an intensive form describing Allah's perfect ability to see and know everything

steps.m: All-Seeing (attribute of Allah, as the subject of this sentence)
steps.n: Added tanwin damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changes from singular verb to plural noun showing multiple worshippers
Added definite article ال and kasra at end to show it's affected by previous word
Added preposition بِ meaning 'with'
This is a basic particle that introduces statements or conditions

steps.m: indeed/truly (emphasizes the statement about الَّذِينَ that follows)
steps.n: Adding the shadda changes this from a conditional particle to an emphatic one. It makes the statement that follows stronger and more certain.
Added ال to make it point to someone specific instead of just any person

steps.m: those who (subject of يَكْفُرُونَ - they disbelieve)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word describes the people who are doing the actions in the ayah (disbelieving, killing prophets, etc.)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense (he rejected) to present tense (he rejects/disbelieves)
Changed from singular (he) to plural (they) by adding ون at the end. Links to 'signs of Allah' that follows it
Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'a sign'
Forms the feminine plural of the word
Adds preposition ب meaning 'with/by'. This word is connected to يَكْفُرُونَ (they disbelieve), showing what they disbelieve in.
Adds kasra at the end to show this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that follows it
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of the word 'signs' (بِـَٔايٰتِ) in 'signs of Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
The يَ prefix changes it from past tense (killed) to present tense (kills)
The ونَ ending shows multiple people are doing the action (in this case, referring to those who disbelieve in the previous part of the ayah)
The وَ connects this to their previous action of disbelieving in Allah's signs mentioned earlier in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic noun
Changed the word to show someone who does this action as their special role
Added 'al' (the) at the start, which made the n sound stronger
Added ين at the end to make it plural (more than one prophet)
Basic noun form showing something that is different or changed
Added بِ at the beginning and ِ at the end to show this describes how something is done wrongfully/without right. The ِ shows it connects to the word حَقٍّ (truth/justice) that comes after it.
Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to make it a strong noun meaning 'truth'

steps.m: any right/justification (being used with بِغَيْرِ meaning 'without any right')
steps.n: Added two dots under the word (tanween) to show it means 'any right' - works with بِغَيْرِ to mean 'without any right'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they kill (referring to the disbelievers mentioned in يَكْفُرُونَ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to the same group mentioned earlier who disbelieve (يَكْفُرُونَ)
Added و to connect this action with the previous one (disbelieving)
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people instead of just one
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the ones' instead of just 'ones'

steps.m: those who (describing people who يَكْفُرُونَ - disbelieve)
steps.n: Added final vowels and changed spelling slightly to match Quran writing style. This word introduces people who disbelieve, connecting to the verb يَكْفُرُونَ (they disbelieve) that comes after it
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it past tense
Adding ي at the start changes it from past (he commanded) to present (he commands)

steps.m: they command (those who kill the prophets)
steps.n: Changed from 'he commands' to 'they command' by adding ون at the end to show multiple people doing the action
Changes the basic idea of being fair into the actual quality or state of fairness
Adding ال makes it refer to justice as a specific concept
Adding ب shows how something is done. Here it connects to the word يَأْمُرُونَ (they command), showing what they command people to do - to act with justice.

steps.m: with the justice (object of يَأْمُرُونَ - they command)
steps.n: The ِ ending shows this word follows the preposition ب. The whole phrase tells us what these people command - they command others to act with justice.
Basic preposition showing source or origin
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word (ٱلنَّاسِ). This helps link the words together smoothly in pronunciation.
Root meaning 'to be social' became the word for 'people' by adding an alif after the first letter
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - 'the people' instead of just 'people'
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is the object of a preposition
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to make the verb stronger and turns it into a command
Added هُم to show who should receive the news

steps.m: so give them news! (command) (connected to يَكْفُرُونَ - they disbelieve)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a result of their previous actions mentioned in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters together and make a basic past-tense verb
Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. The word is in genitive case because it comes after the preposition بِ.
Adding بِ at the beginning shows this punishment is being used as a tool or means
The tanwin (ٍ) shows this is any punishment in general, not a specific one. This word is connected to فَبَشِّرْهُم (then give them news) in the ayah.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this describes something that causes intense pain. This word describes عَذَابٍ (punishment) in this ayah.
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is describing something else (the punishment) in a general way
This is the basic form of the demonstrative pronoun that points to multiple people or things
Adding ئِ shows that what we're pointing to is far away

steps.m: those (pointing to people far away) (subject of حَبِطَتْ)
steps.n: Adding كَ completes the pointing action. This word is the subject of حَبِطَتْ (became worthless) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one person

steps.m: to fail, become worthless, or go to waste

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past (subject of أَعْمٰلُهُمْ - their deeds)
Added ت to show this refers to أَعْمٰلُهُمْ (their deeds), which is feminine in Arabic
Adding vowels creates the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (doing) to plural noun (multiple deeds) using the common pattern أفعال for plurals
The 'a' sound is stretched longer and ends with an 'u' sound

steps.m: their deeds (subject of حَبِطَتْ - became worthless)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show possession. This word is the subject of حَبِطَتْ (became worthless) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows location or being inside something
The ى extends the meaning to show being fully within something. Here it shows being completely in both this world and the hereafter.
Root د-ن-و means 'near' - added يا at the end to make it mean 'the nearest/worldly life' (as opposed to the afterlife)
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific, and doubled the د (shown by ّ) because it comes after ٱلْ

steps.m: the worldly life (acted upon by حَبِطَتْ)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb حَبِطَتْ (became worthless)
Changed the verb into a word describing something that comes after or is later

steps.m: the life that comes after (this life)
steps.n: Added ة to make it feminine and specify it refers to the afterlife specifically
Added و (and) and ال (the) at start, and ِ at end to show this word is connected to الدُّنْيَا (this world) mentioned before it
Basic negative particle ما with fatha added for pronunciation

steps.m: and not (there are no helpers for them)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. This connects to لَهُم (for them) which comes after it in the ayah.
Added fatha to show the basic meaning of direction or purpose
Added هُم which shows this applies to a group of people (them)
Basic preposition indicating source or origin

The meem gets a shadda because it comes before the word ناصرين which starts with ن. When two similar sounds meet like this in Arabic, they often merge together, making the first letter stronger (with shadda).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb (he helped) to a noun meaning 'helper' by using the pattern فاعِل. This pattern is used to describe someone who does the action.
Adds ين at the end to make it mean 'many helpers' instead of just one helper
Adds ِ before ين to show it comes after a word like مِن
This is the basic negation word that changes a present tense verb to past tense negative
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question that expects 'yes' as an answer

steps.m: to see/observe with eyes or mind

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed from past to present tense by adding تَ prefix

steps.m: you see (after أَلَمْ meaning 'have you not seen?')
steps.n: The word is shortened by dropping أى at the end because it comes after أَلَمْ (haven't you) - this is a common pattern in questions
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'they are called' (يُدْعَوْنَ) with what they are called to - 'the book' (كِتٰبِ).
This is the basic singular form of the relative pronoun that means 'who' or 'which'
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ال at the beginning to make it definite, changing it from just 'who' to 'those who'
Added final fatha to show this is the subject of the verb يُدْعَوْنَ (they are called/invited) in this ayah. Also added shadda on ل and changed ا to ٱ for proper pronunciation.
Changed to passive voice to show receiving rather than giving
Added وا at the end to show multiple people received something. This word is the subject of يُدْعَوْنَ (they are called) in this ayah.
Changes from verb 'to establish' to noun meaning 'an established portion'

steps.m: a share/portion (object of أُوتُوا - were given)
steps.n: Added ًا (an) at the end to show this word is receiving the action (it's what was given)
Basic preposition meaning 'from' - one of the most common Arabic prepositions
Added shadda because this word connects to the next word (الْكِتٰبِ). The shadda shows this connection by making the 'م' sound stronger.
Added fatha at the end to help connect this word smoothly to the next word in speech
Changed the root into a noun that means the result of writing - a book or scripture
Adding ال makes the word specific/definite
The kasra at the end shows this word comes after مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the letters and form past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The weak letter و becomes ى at the end.

steps.m: they are called/invited (referring to a group being called to Allah's Book)
steps.n: Added ون (which becomes وْنَ) to make it plural. The ى changes back to و before the plural ending.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to the word كِتٰبِ (book) to show movement towards or reference to it.
Added a small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's an elongated alif, which helps with proper pronunciation
Changed from an action to the thing that results from that action - something written down. In this ayah, it refers specifically to divine scripture.
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an alif khanjariya, which is just a different way of writing the same sound
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'the Book of Allah'
Added shadda to emphasize the L sound, showing this is a special name
Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to something. Here it shows this is Allah's book (كِتٰبِ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added لِ to show purpose and changed final vowel to fatha to show this is part of a purpose clause

steps.m: to be separate or distinct from something else

steps.n: Changes from verb root to noun meaning 'between'
Added a mark to show this word is being acted upon by the verb يَحْكُمَ (to judge)

steps.m: between them (being acted upon by يَحْكُمَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'them' (referring to multiple males or a mixed group)
This word shows that something happens after something else. In this ayah, it shows that after they are invited to Allah's Book (يُدْعَوْنَ), then a group of them turns away.

steps.m: to be close to or take charge of something

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a past tense verb by adding the vowel marks 'fatha' (َ) to make 'he took charge'
Adding shadda makes the action stronger or more complete
Adding تَ shows the person is doing the action to themselves
Adding يَ at the start shows the action is happening now or in the future. The ى with alif above (ىٰ) at the end is because this is a special kind of word that ends with a weak letter.

steps.m: to split or divide into parts
a separated group (that has split off)
steps.n: Adding ي between second and third root letters turns this into a noun meaning 'something that embodies splitting/separating'. Here it refers to a group that has separated itself.

steps.m: a group (that has split off from others)
steps.n: The ٌ (tanween) shows this is talking about any group, not a specific one
Added sukun to show end of basic preposition
Added هُمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them' (masculine plural)
Added shadda on first letter for pronunciation emphasis after previous word ending in vowel
This is a pronoun that refers to a group of people - specifically here it refers to those who were given a portion of the Book
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to what came before, showing these actions happen in sequence - they are called to Allah's Book and then they turn away

steps.m: to turn away or avoid something

steps.n: Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who does the action of turning away
Added ون to show this refers to multiple people turning away
The doubled م adds extra strength to the meaning of turning away
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written as a small mark above the ذ to show it's a special form
Adding لِ changes the meaning from pointing to something near to pointing to something far
Adding كَ completes the word and reinforces that we're pointing to something far away
This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it, like saying 'indeed' or 'that' in English
Adding بِ changes the meaning to show reason or cause, connecting it to what came before (ذٰلِكَ - that)

steps.m: because they (connected to قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows who the word is talking about - in this case, it refers to the same people who are the subject of قَالُوا (they said) later in the ayah
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. This shows the action happened in the past. The و changes to ا because it's between two vowels.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action instead of just one person (he)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it impossible in the future. Here it negates يَفْتَرُونَ (they invent), showing their claims will definitely not happen.
The root letters م-س-س were joined together and since there were two س letters next to each other, they combined into one س with a shadda (doubled)
Adding تَ at the beginning changes it from 'he touched' to 'being touched' - like the difference between 'I hit' and 'I got hit'

steps.m: it will touch/affect us (لَن makes it negative future)
steps.n: Adding نَا at the end shows this happens to 'us'. The word لَن before it makes it mean 'will not' happen in the future.
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) between the root letters to make it pronounceable
The middle letter و changes to ا to make it easier to say. The word becomes more specific - now it only means fire instead of light in general.
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific fire. The ن gets a shadda because it comes after ال.
The ُ (damma) shows this word is the subject of the sentence
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning of exception

Added elongated alif at the end. This is a spelling variation that shows the word connects to what comes after it (أَيَّامًا meaning 'days'). The meaning is that they will only be touched by fire for a few days.
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the basic word for 'day'
Changed to plural by adding أ at start and making the middle longer with ي - like saying 'dayyy' instead of 'day'
The two ي letters combine into one stronger ي with shadda
Added tanween alif to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to show something that received the action of counting
Added the feminine plural ending to show multiple counted things
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter
Added the two dots at the end (tanwin) to show we're talking about specific counted days or things
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show intense deception. The subject 'he' refers to what deceived them (their false beliefs) in this context.
Added و to connect this with the previous part of the ayah where they claimed the fire won't touch them
Added attached pronoun هُمْ meaning 'them' as the object
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to 'their religion' (دِينِهِم) in the ayah to show what they were 'in'.

steps.m: to follow a path or system

steps.n: The kasra and long vowel ي create a noun meaning the system or path that someone follows
The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it
Adding هِم shows this religion belongs to them (referring to the people mentioned earlier in the verse)
This is the basic form of the word that means 'what' or 'that which'. It connects different parts of a sentence, like saying 'what they were' in English.

steps.m: what/that which (emphasizing the connection to يَفْتَرُونَ - they were inventing)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show this word strongly connects to the verb that follows it (يَفْتَرُونَ - they were inventing).
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the middle.
Adding وا at the end makes it mean 'they' instead of 'he'. This word connects to يَفْتَرُونَ (they invent) by showing who is doing the inventing.

steps.m: to make up or fabricate falsely

steps.n: Connected root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added reflexive pattern to show doing action for oneself
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels inside the word
Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha on ك and sukun on ي
The prefix فَ connects this question to what was said before, like saying 'so' in English

steps.m: when (followed by جَمَعْنٰهُمْ - we gather them)
steps.n: This is a particle that means 'when' specifically for future or conditional events. In this ayah, it introduces what will happen when Allah gathers people on the Day of Judgment.

steps.m: to gather, collect, or bring together

steps.n: Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action

steps.m: we gathered them (referring to the people who will be gathered on judgment day)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who was gathered. The alif (ٰ) before هُمْ is just to help with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The middle letter و stays as a consonant.
Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: for a day (referring to يَوْمٍ mentioned in 3:25)
steps.n: Added tanween (double kasra) to show this is 'a day' - not a specific day
This is the basic negative particle used to say 'no' or 'not' to something
The shadda (double letter) makes the negation stronger. In this ayah, it's saying there is absolutely no doubt about something.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to transform the concept into a noun meaning 'doubt'
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by لا (no). The phrase لا ريب means 'there is no doubt'.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment - like being inside or within something

steps.m: in it (referring to يَوْمٍ - that Day)
steps.n: Added هِ ('it/him') at the end to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to يَوْمٍ (that Day) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense voweling
Changed from active (he fulfilled) to passive (was fulfilled) by changing vowels
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning

steps.m: she/it was completely fulfilled/fully paid (passive, emphatic)
steps.n: Adds feminine marker to passive verb

steps.m: and she/it was completely fulfilled/fully paid (passive, emphatic) (connected to كُلُّ نَفْسٍ)
steps.n: Added conjunction و to connect to previous phrase. This word refers to كُلُّ نَفْسٍ (every soul) which is feminine, explaining the feminine form.
Added damma (ُ) to make it a noun meaning 'all' or 'every', and doubled the middle letter to make the meaning stronger. This word describes نَفْسٍ (soul) in the ayah, meaning 'every soul'.
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this word is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: to breathe, be alive, have spirit

steps.n: Basic noun pattern with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter creates a noun meaning 'soul' or 'self'
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite ('a soul' not 'the soul') and is being acted upon by the verb وُفِّيَتْ (was given in full)
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb كَسَبَتْ (earned/gained), showing what was earned
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
The م gets doubled (shadda) because it comes right after a word ending in ـمٍ, making the م sound stronger when reading

steps.m: to gain, earn, or acquire through effort

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added ت at the end to show the subject is feminine (نَفْسٍ/soul)
This is a pronoun that means 'they' for a group of males or mixed group. It acts as the subject of the verb لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (they will not be wronged) in the next part of the ayah.
The وَ at the start means 'and', connecting this clause to what came before. The whole phrase وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ means 'and they will not be wronged'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it mean the opposite. In this ayah, it makes يُظْلَمُونَ (they are wronged) into 'they are not wronged'.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation

steps.m: to treat unfairly or harm someone's rights

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this is happening to someone rather than someone doing it
Changed from past tense to present tense by adding يُ at the start, with a sukun to make it easier to say

steps.m: they are being treated unfairly (in لَا يُظْلَمُونَ - they will not be treated unfairly)
steps.n: Added ون to show this is happening to multiple people. This word appears with لَا (not) in the ayah, showing they will not be treated unfairly.
The root letters change to make a command - the first letter gets a damma (ُ) and the middle letter is dropped, making it shorter and easier to say

steps.m: say! (command to one person, before اللَّهُمَّ)
steps.n: The ِ (kasra) is added at the end because this command is connected to what comes next (اللَّهُمَّ). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be devoted to, to worship

steps.n: Changed from root meaning 'worship' to noun meaning 'something that is worshipped'
Added ال (the) to show this is THE one true God, not just any deity
Added shadda to emphasize the middle letter, making it specific to Allah
Added damma to show this is the subject
Added مّ to call out to Allah directly, like saying 'O Allah!'
Added مَ to make it a calling/invocation of Allah

steps.m: to own or rule over something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the pattern to show who does the action by adding alif after first letter and kasra under second letter
Changed alif to alif with bar (مٰ) and added fatha at end to show connection to the word after it (الملك)
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the thing that is owned/ruled
Added 'the' at the beginning and kasra at the end to show this is the object being possessed in the phrase 'Owner of the kingdom'
Connected the root letters together
تُ at start shows it's happening now and means 'you'
The ْ (sukun) shows there's no vowel between first two root letters
Changed the root into a noun by adding ُ (u-sound) on first letter and ْ (no vowel) on second letter. This changes it from an action (to rule) into a thing (kingdom/power)
Added ٱلْ (al-) at the beginning to mean 'the', just like in English

steps.m: the kingdom/power (object of تُؤْتِى - You give)
steps.n: Added َ (a-sound) at the end to show this is what is being given. In this verse, Allah is telling us He gives this kingdom/power to whom He wills.
This is a special word used to refer to people in a general way. In this ayah, it's being used to show who receives the action of giving (تُؤْتِى), meaning 'to whomever You give...'

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Added vowels and the weak letter ا replaced ي to make the word easier to say
Added تَ at start to show 'you' are doing it now, and changed the ending to ُ to show it's happening in the present
Changed ا to آ (long alif) to show the extended sound in the word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form past tense verb
Changed from past to present by adding تَ at start to mean 'you', with different vowel marks to show it's happening now

steps.m: and you take away (referring to Allah taking away kingdom/authority from whom He wills)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect with previous phrase about giving kingdom. This shows Allah's complete power over giving and taking away authority.
Adding ُ on first letter and ْ on second letter changes the verb into a noun meaning the place or thing that is ruled

steps.m: the kingdom (object of تُؤْتِى - you give)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific (the kingdom), and َ at the end shows it's receiving the action of giving
This is a question word used to ask about people, like saying 'who?' in English
Different word - 'man' (who) and 'min' (from) are separate words, not a transformation

steps.m: from whom (connected to تَشَآءُ - you will)
steps.n: When مِن comes before مَن, the ن and م combine to make a stronger م sound (shown by ّ). This word connects to تَشَآءُ (you will) to mean 'from whom you will'

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Added fathas and the weak letter ا replaced ي to make the past tense form
Adding ت at start makes it 'you' or 'she' instead of 'he'
The ُ at the end shows this is happening now (present tense)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added shadda to make the meaning stronger
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by adding تُ at start, and made it present tense by changing the middle vowels

steps.m: and you honor greatly (connected to تُؤْتِى and تَنزِعُ)
steps.n: Added و to connect this action with the previous actions in the verse
This is a special word that refers to people (not things). In this ayah, it shows who receives the kingdom - Allah gives the kingdom to whoever He wants.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Changed the middle letter ي to ا and added vowels to make 'he wanted' in the past tense
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed ending to ُ to show present tense. This word shows who receives the giving (تُؤْتِى) in this ayah.
Added ا in middle to show this is a special type of verb with a 'hollow' middle
Combined the doubled letters with a shadda
Added damma at end to show indicative mood

steps.m: and you make low (connected to وَتُعِزُّ - and you honor)
steps.n: This word is connected to وَتُعِزُّ (and you honor) in the verse, showing the contrast between honoring and humbling
This is a special word that refers to people (not things). In this ayah, it shows who is receiving something - Allah gives authority to whoever He wants.
Adding the fatha (َ) at the end makes it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: you/she want(s) (subject of تُؤْتِى - you give)
steps.n: Adding تَ at the start changes it to present tense and shows who is doing the action. This word is connected to تُؤْتِى (you give) in the ayah, showing who is doing the giving.
The diacritical marks (tashkeel) were removed except for the final damma
This is a special word meaning 'hand' that only has two letters instead of the usual three
The بِ shows this is about using or wielding power

steps.m: in Your control/power (referring to Allah's absolute power mentioned in الخير)
steps.n: Adding كَ to show this refers to Allah's power. This connects to الخير (the good) which refers to Allah's absolute control mentioned throughout this ayah about giving and taking away authority.
Changed the root into a noun by adding vowel marks. In this ayah, it refers to all good things and benefits that are in Allah's control.
The word gets 'the' in front (ال) and becomes specific goodness
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. In this ayah, it emphasizes Allah's power and ability.
The كَ at the end means 'You' when speaking to Allah or one male person with respect. Here it's addressing Allah directly.
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of, upon, or over something else. It doesn't come from a root.
Added the small alif (ٰ) to show this is connected to the word after it (كُلِّ). This mark shows the alif is elongated in pronunciation.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word is connected to شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'of every thing'
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Added the genitive case ending because this word follows كُلِّ (every/all) and describes what Allah has power over in this ayah
The ي between د and ر shows this is a permanent and complete quality
The ٌ shows this is describing Allah's attribute of complete power over everything mentioned in the ayah (giving authority, taking it away, honoring, and humbling)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif at start to show making something happen
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: You make (something) enter (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'You' by replacing يُ with تُ. In this ayah, this is addressing Allah directly.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a proper noun

steps.m: the night (object of تُولِجُ - you make enter)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it's the object being acted upon. The word is being affected by the verb تُولِجُ (you make enter).
Basic preposition form showing location or containment. In this ayah, it connects the verb تُولِجُ (you cause to enter) with ٱلنَّهَارِ (the day).
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show it's a preposition
Changed the final ي to ى which is common at the end of words
Connected the root letters with vowels to create basic word form
Adding ا makes it mean 'intense brightness/flowing' which refers to daylight
Added 'the' (ال) at the start, which causes the ن to be doubled
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after the preposition فِى (into) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added alif at start to show causing someone else to do the action
Changed to present tense 'you' form by adding تُ

steps.m: and she causes to enter (الليل into النهار)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this word to the previous one. This word shows Allah making the night merge into the day.
Connected the root letters with vowels to form basic noun
Added alif after second root letter to create noun for the time when brightness flows

steps.m: the day (object of تُولِجُ - you make enter)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن because it comes after ال

steps.m: the day (object of تُولِجُ - you make enter)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb تُولِجُ (you make enter)
This is a preposition showing that the night enters into the day. It helps connect the verb تُولِجُ (you cause to enter) with where it enters ٱلنَّهَارِ (the day)
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun
Added ٱل to make it specific. The ل gets a shadda because it merges with the first letter of the word
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِي (in) in this ayah
Adding alif at start makes it causative - showing someone making something else do the action

steps.m: you (sing. masc.) bring out / she brings out
steps.n: Changes to present tense and makes it either 'you' (male) or 'she'

steps.m: and you/she makes (something) exit (subject of تَرْزُقُ)
steps.n: Added و to connect this verb with the previous phrase. This word is connected to تَرْزُقُ (provides) later in the verse.
Root letters of 'life' connected with short 'a' vowels to make basic past tense verb
The two ي letters combine into one doubled letter with shadda, making the meaning stronger

steps.m: the Living One (object of تُخْرِجُ - you bring out)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it definite and specific. In this ayah, it's being acted upon by the verb تُخْرِجُ (you bring out)

steps.m: the Living One (object of تُخْرِجُ - you bring out)
steps.n: The ي is written as ى when it has a shadda at the end of a word
This is the basic preposition that shows something coming from or out of something else
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word ٱلْحَىِّ (the living). This shows that something (the dead) is coming from the living.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a noun meaning 'death'
Changed و to ي and added shadda to show something that has experienced death
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
Added kasra at the end because this word follows مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding alif at the start makes it causative - showing someone making something else do the action

steps.m: you (masculine) make come out / she makes come out
steps.n: Changes to present tense and makes it either 'you' (male) or 'she'

steps.m: and she makes (something) come out (subject of تُولِجُ)
steps.n: Added و at start to connect this verb with the previous verb تُولِجُ in the ayah
Changes from abstract 'death' to describing something that is dead
Added shadda and kasra to intensify the meaning
Added ال (with hamzat wasl ٱ) to make it specific

steps.m: the dead one (object of تُخْرِجُ - you bring out)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb تُخْرِجُ (you bring out)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it shows that the living comes from/out of the dead.
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word (ٱلْمَيِّتِ - the dead). This is a common change when مِن comes before ال.
Root 'to live' becomes 'the living' by adding ال (the) at the start
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense with 'you' as the subject. The ُ at the end shows this is the main verb.

steps.m: and you provide (connected to previous actions in ayah)
steps.n: Added و to connect this action with the previous actions mentioned (entering night into day, bringing forth living from dead, etc.)
This is a special word that refers to people in a general way. In this ayah, it shows who is receiving Allah's provision - meaning 'whoever You want' or 'anyone You choose to provide for'.
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it 'he wanted' in the past tense
The weak letter hamza (ء) combines with alif to make a different spelling
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed end vowel to ُ to show present tense
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) when it comes after a fatha
Root letters connected with vowels (َ and ْ) to make a word meaning 'other than' or 'different from'
Added preposition بِ to show 'without' or 'other than'
Added kasra at the end to show connection to the following word حِسَابٍ (reckoning)
Changed from action to the result of that action (like changing 'calculate' to 'calculation')

steps.m: any calculation/reckoning (in بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ - without any reckoning)
steps.n: Added two dots under the last letter to show 'any' calculation/reckoning, making it part of 'without any reckoning'
This is a negative particle that tells us something should not happen. Here it's telling the believers they should not take the disbelievers as allies.

steps.m: absolutely not (negating يَتَّخِذِ - should not take)
steps.n: The shadda (doubled L sound) makes the negation stronger, emphasizing that believers should absolutely not take disbelievers as allies
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added ت with shadda and changed initial vowel to show reflexive meaning (doing something for oneself)
Changed to present tense by adding يَ prefix and changing vowels
Changed to jussive mood by adding kasra at the end
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb about feeling secure
Added an alif at start to show someone actively choosing to believe/trust in something
Changed to active participle form showing someone who does the action
Added sound masculine plural ending
Added definite article to specify these particular believers. This word is the subject of the verb يَتَّخِذِ (take/adopt) in this ayah.

steps.m: to reject or deny truth by covering it up

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he rejected' in the past
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Makes the word plural to show multiple people
Adds 'the' to specify these particular rejecters. In this verse, this word is the object of يَتَّخِذِ (take as allies/protectors).

steps.m: to be close to and protect/support

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a past tense verb meaning 'to protect'
Changed from single protector to plural protectors by adding hamza at start and lengthening the middle
Added fatha at end to show this word is the object of the verb يَتَّخِذِ (take/adopt)

steps.m: from/of (connects دُونِ with what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a helper word that shows where something starts or what group it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects the word دُونِ (instead of/besides) to what comes before it.

steps.m: to be lower than or beneath something

steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) changes it to mean 'beneath/lower'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word comes after مِن (from) in the phrase مِن دُونِ المؤمنين (besides/instead of the believers)
Adding م prefix and changing vowel pattern to create noun of agent/participle form
Adding ين at the end makes this refer to many believers. This word is being acted upon by the verb يَتَّخِذِ (take/adopt).
Adding ٱلْ at the start points to specific believers, not just any believers. This word is being acted upon by the verb يَتَّخِذِ (take/adopt).

steps.m: whoever (introduces a conditional statement in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a special word that introduces a condition - it means 'whoever' when talking about what happens if someone does something

steps.m: and whoever (connects this conditional phrase to the previous statement)
steps.n: The و at the beginning connects this condition to the previous statement about believers not taking disbelievers as allies
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present/future tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action

steps.m: he does (in conditional phrase with مَن)
steps.n: Changed the ending to sukun because this word comes after مَن (whoever) in a conditional phrase - 'whoever does that'
Starting with the basic demonstrative pronoun ذا which points to something nearby
Adding the alif above (ٰ) changes it to point to something farther away
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) adds emphasis and helps point to something distant
Adding ك with fatha (كَ) completes the word, making it point to something specific but distant
This is a special verb that only comes in past tense form but gives present meaning. It's used to negate things.
Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete verb meaning 'is not'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before it in the verse
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something. Here it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to show separation or distance from Allah.
Added a fatha (َ) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word اللَّهِ. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the name for God in Arabic.
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection, and added shadda to emphasize the L sound. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the same sound as ا.

steps.m: in (connects شَىْءٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects the word شَىْءٍ (thing) to the previous part of the sentence.

steps.m: in (connects شَىْءٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'

steps.m: in (connects شَىْءٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: Changed the final ي to ى which is just a different way of writing the same sound
Root letters got vowels to make the common noun meaning 'thing'
Added the genitive case ending because it comes after the preposition مِنَ (from/of)
This is the basic form of the exceptive particle that excludes what comes after it from what was mentioned before
Added shadda on ل to show emphasis in pronunciation

steps.m: except (excluding what follows from لَّا يَتَّخِذِ - do not take)
steps.n: Extended alif (ا → آ) shows a pause in recitation. Here it introduces the exception to the prohibition against taking disbelievers as allies.

steps.m: that (connects to تَتَّقُوا۟ - you protect yourselves)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that connects two ideas. Here it connects the exception (إِلَّآ) with what you can do (protect yourselves).
Connected letters with fatha vowels to make past tense. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added اِتَّ at start to show doing action to oneself carefully. The و changes to ت and combines with the other ت.
Changed اِ to تَ at the start to show 'you' instead of 'he' is doing the action
Added وا at the end to show multiple people, and the ى changes because of this
Added sukun to show the end of the word part
Added هُم at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to the word الكافرين (the disbelievers) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Changes verb 'protect' into feminine noun meaning 'protection'
Changed ا to ىٰ because it's a weak letter word
Added ً to show this is the object of the verb تَتَّقُوا (to protect against)

steps.m: to warn or be cautious about danger

steps.n: Added shadda to show strong warning
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels inside the word
Added كُمُ to show who is being warned

steps.m: and he warns you all strongly
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this word to what came before it
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يُحَذِّرُ - warns)
steps.n: Added the ُ to show this is the subject doing the warning. Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes in middle of speech.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
The fatha shows this word is receiving the action (it's what is being warned about)
Added هُ with a long vowel marker (ۥ) to show this refers to Allah warning about Himself
Adds the conjunction 'and' while preserving the base meaning 'to/towards'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of مِنَ (from). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.

steps.m: to become, to arrive at a destination
steps.n: This root carries the basic idea of movement toward an end point or transformation
Adding مَ at the start turns the action into a place where that action happens
Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - not just any destination, but a particular one
The ُ shows this word is the subject of its sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to a command form by: 1) Dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) Adding damma on first letter, 3) Adding sukun on last letter. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak.

steps.m: if (introduces the condition 'you hide what is in your hearts or reveal it')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a possible situation. In this ayah, it connects the condition (hiding or revealing what's in your hearts) with its result (Allah knows it).
Basic past tense verb showing something was hidden
Adding تُ changes it to present tense and makes 'you' the doer
Changed from 'you/she hides' to 'you all hide' by adding وا at the end - this makes it plural (more than one person)
This word connects to فِى صُدُورِكُمْ (in your chests/hearts) to mean 'whatever is in your hearts'. It's used here to refer to all thoughts and feelings people might hide.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. In this ayah, it connects what is being hidden (ما) with where it's being hidden (صُدُورِ - chests/hearts).
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed the vowels to make it plural
Added كُمْ to show the chests belong to multiple people being spoken to

This is a basic conjunction that gives a choice between two options. In this ayah, it connects 'hide' (تُخْفُوا) with 'reveal' (تُبْدُوهُ), showing that whether you do one or the other, Allah knows it.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into past tense form
Changed to present tense form with 'you' as the doer

steps.m: you reveal it (object of يَعْلَمْ - Allah knows it)
steps.n: Added هُ to show what is being revealed. This word is the object that Allah knows in the phrase يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ (Allah knows it)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show present tense. Added sukun at end to show it connects to something after it.
Added هُ at the end to show 'it' as the thing being known
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on middle letter, and ُ at end
Added وَ (and) to connect this with the previous statement about Allah knowing what's in your hearts
This is a special word that connects to فِى to show 'what is in' something. Here it's asking about what's inside صُدُورِكُمْ (your chests/hearts).
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows location. Here it shows where something is - specifically what is in their chests.
Added kasra (ِ) under the ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'
Changed the ي to ى which is just a different way of writing the same sound at the end of a word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun. The و changes to ا at the end.
Added وات to make it plural. The و returns because it's no longer at the end.
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ٱل
Added kasra at the end because it comes after في (in). The alifs are written as long alifs (ٰ) in this style.
This is a relative pronoun that introduces what is in the heavens, connecting it to the previous statement about Allah knowing
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase about 'what is in the heavens' with the previous statement

steps.m: in (what is in صُدُورِكُمْ - your chests)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it's used to talk about what is 'in' people's chests (hearts/minds). The word فِى connects صُدُورِكُمْ (your chests) to ما (what) to show where something is located.
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to form the basic noun
Adding ٱلْ makes it refer to the specific earth/land. This word comes after فِي (in/on) in the ayah.
The kasra at the end shows this word comes after فِي (in/on)
This is the unique proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity/worship but is a special word that only refers to the One God.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَعْلَمُ - He knows)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the doer of the action (knowing)

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject of يَعْلَمُ - He knows)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this to the previous statement, plus alif with hamza wasl (ٱ) which helps pronunciation

This is a preposition that connects قَدِيرٌ (capable/able) to what it has power over. The small alif (ىٰ) at the end is just for writing - it's pronounced like a regular alif. In this ayah, it shows Allah has power over all things.
The doubled ل shows this means absolutely everything. This word describes شَىْءٍ (thing) in this ayah.
Adding kasra (ِ) shows this connects to the word after it (شَىْءٍ meaning 'thing'), like saying 'of all' or 'every' in English

steps.m: to be a thing or exist

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and sukun to make the common word for 'thing'
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is owned by كُلِّ (every/all), forming the phrase 'every thing'.

steps.m: to be able, to have power over something

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a strong, permanent quality. This pattern is often used for Allah's attributes.

steps.m: All-Powerful, having absolute ability over everything (describing اللَّهُ)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this is a complete description of Allah's power, not limited or defined by anything
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و takes a sukun because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: the day (when تَجِدُ each soul will find)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb تَجِدُ (will find) that comes after it in the ayah. It means this is when the finding will happen.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و drops because it's a weak letter at the start.
Changed from masculine to feminine by replacing يَ with تَ. This word refers to كُلُّ نَفْسٍ (every soul) which is feminine in Arabic.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning more complete/total
Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject of what follows.
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to create the noun form
Added tanwin kasra to show this is an indefinite noun being acted upon. In this ayah, each soul is being found/discovered with what it did.
Basic form of the relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence, like saying 'what' in 'what she did'
Added emphasis by doubling the م with a shadda. This is a common pronunciation change after certain words, but doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense verb

steps.m: she did (referring to نَفْسٍ - a soul)
steps.n: Added ت to show the subject is feminine (نَفْسٍ - a soul) in this ayah

steps.m: from (connects خَيْرٍ to what was done)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or what it's made of. Here it connects the word خَيْرٍ (good) to what was done.
Root meaning 'good' becomes a simple noun meaning 'goodness'
Added tanwin kasratin to show this is the object of the preposition مِنْ (from). In the ayah, it's talking about finding what good deeds someone did.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show something is being brought/presented by someone else, not coming on its own
Added tanween fatha to show this word is receiving the action
Added shadda on م to connect with previous word in pronunciation
This is a relative pronoun referring to the evil deeds mentioned in the ayah. It connects with عَمِلَتْ (she did/worked) to mean 'what was done'.

steps.m: and what (that was done of evil)
