The و makes it mean 'someone who does this action a lot'
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. The ل combines with ر to make رّ. This word is what was followed in وَٱتَّبَعْنَا (and we followed).

steps.m: the messenger (as the one being followed)
steps.n: The َ (fatha) at the end shows this is the object - the one being followed
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes 'he wrote' into 'write!' by adding U- at start and changing vowels - makes it a command
The suffix نَا ('us') is added to the command 'write!' to show who is being written about - it means 'write us!'

steps.m: so write us! (in response to رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا - Our Lord, we have believed)
steps.n: Adds فَ (so/then) to show this is a response to 'Our Lord, we have believed'
This is a preposition that shows being together with something. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلشَّاهِدِينَ (the witnesses) to show 'with the witnesses'

steps.m: to witness or testify about what you've seen

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action
Added ين to show there are many witnesses. This ending is used because the word comes after مَعَ (with)
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the ش letter. The word follows مَعَ (with) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word appears in a pair of actions - first the disbelievers plotted, then Allah plotted in response.
Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this action together
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it. Also added the small و mark (۟) above the final alif to show this is a complete thought.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense. This word is connected to خَيْرُ (best) in this ayah.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the ayah
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity and worship, but has become a unique proper noun.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation

steps.m: Allah (as the doer of مَكَرَ - plotted)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action in this sentence

steps.m: and Allah (as the doer of مَكَرَ - plotted)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement

steps.m: and Allah (as the doer of مَكَرَ - plotted)
steps.n: Changed initial alif to a waṣla form since it comes after another word
Changes the word into a noun meaning 'good/goodness'
Added damma (u sound) at the end to show this word means 'best' and connects to describing Allah
The root م-ك-ر meaning 'to plot' becomes مَاكِر meaning 'plotter' by adding an alif after the first letter and a kasra under the third letter - this makes it describe the person doing the action

steps.m: the plotters/planners (best of them is اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added 'ال' (the) at the start and changed the ending to 'ين' to show it's plural - used in the famous phrase 'Allah is the best of planners'
This is a time particle that introduces a past event. In this ayah, it introduces when Allah spoke to Jesus. It helps connect this statement to the previous context.

steps.m: he said (introducing what Allah says to Jesus)
steps.n: Added fatha vowels to show this happened in the past. The و changes to ا due to Arabic pronunciation rules.
Changed the root into a noun form meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Changed to the proper name of God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of قَالَ (said).
Adding vowel marks to properly pronounce the name Jesus
Added يٰ at the beginning to show we're calling out to Jesus. This is like saying 'Hey Jesus!' or 'O Jesus!' in English

steps.m: O Jesus! (calling out to Jesus with emphasis)
steps.n: Added a long vowel mark (ٰ) and pause mark (ٓ) at the end to show emphasis when calling out
This is the basic emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain, like adding 'truly' in English
Added ي at the end to show this is about me (I/my). The shadda (ّ) shows strong emphasis. This word connects to مُتَوَفِّيكَ (taking you) to show who is doing the taking.
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: to be fulfilled/to receive in full
steps.n: Adding تَ at the beginning shows someone/something is receiving or taking something completely (like a soul being taken at death)
Adding مُ prefix changes the verb 'to receive' into 'one who receives' - like changing 'eat' to 'eater'
Changed from passive (being taken) to active (one who takes), with shadda staying the same

steps.m: one who is taking/receiving you (object of إِنِّى)
steps.n: Adds ك to show you are the one being taken/received. This word is the object of إِنِّى (indeed I) in this ayah.

steps.m: to lift up or raise something higher

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Added the pronoun 'you' to show who is being raised. In this ayah, Allah is telling Jesus that He will raise him.
Added 'and' to connect this with Allah's previous statement about taking Jesus
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination

steps.m: to me (connected to مَرْجِعُكُمْ - your return)
steps.n: Added the first person pronoun ي with shadda (ّ) to show strong connection to 'me'. This word connects to مَرْجِعُكُمْ (your return) to show where the return is to.
Basic meaning of cleansing or purification
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and doubled middle letter with kasra to show someone who does this action strongly
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
Added ُكَ at end to show it belongs to 'you'
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added َ (fatha) at the end to connect smoothly with the next word الَّذِينَ (those who). This helps with pronunciation when reading.
Added the definite article ال which combines with ذي, causing the ل to have a shadda. This makes it specific.
Added ين at the end to make it plural. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in the ayah, describing who did the action.
Added fathas to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this action
Basic past tense form showing someone did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action by adding alif after first letter, kasra after second letter, and damma at the end
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it in the verse
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one
Adding ٱلْ with a shadda makes it point to specific people. In this ayah, it refers to those who disbelieved in Jesus.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added اِتْ at the start to show doing the action to/for oneself - like choosing to follow something
The two ت letters combine into one stronger ت with shadda
The و indicates the subject is 'they' (3rd person masculine plural)

steps.m: they followed you (subject of جَاعِلُ - making/placing)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to show who was followed. This word connects to جَاعِلُ (making/placing) in the ayah, showing these followers are being elevated above the disbelievers.
This root carries the basic meaning of being in a higher position
Connected the letters and added fatha and sukun to make it a word showing location

steps.m: above (in relation to الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا - those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it in the sentence. Here it shows that the followers will be above those who disbelieved.
This is a special word used to connect parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'which' in English
The suffix ين changes the pronoun from singular to plural

steps.m: to reject, deny, or cover up truth
steps.n: These letters together represent the basic concept of denying or covering up truth
Adding the vowels creates the past tense verb meaning 'he disbelieved'
Adding وا at the end shows that a group of people (they) did the action instead of just one person (he)

steps.m: they rejected/disbelieved (referring to those who rejected Jesus)
steps.n: Added pronunciation marks (the small و above the alif and the small circle) that don't change the meaning
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. It's one of the essential words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: 'i' sound under إ and 'a' sound on ل
Added small alif (ٰ) to show the long 'aa' sound is maintained at the end. This word connects to يَوْمِ (day) in this ayah.
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun

steps.m: of day (part of يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيٰمَةِ - Day of Resurrection)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the next word ٱلْقِيٰمَةِ (resurrection) to form 'Day of Resurrection'
This is a conjunction that shows sequence of events with a time gap
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes verb 'stood' into noun meaning 'the act of standing'
Added ة to specify one particular instance of rising, which came to mean specifically the resurrection
Added ال to make it specific - the resurrection
Changed alif to hamza-wasl, combined alif with fatha into ٰ, and added kasra at end to show connection to the word يَوْمِ (day of)

steps.m: then (connects to إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ - to Me is your return)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is a connecting word that joins events in sequence, showing what happens next. Here it connects the previous statements about Jesus to what happens after - the return to Allah.

steps.m: then (with emphasis, connects to إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ - to Me is your return)
steps.n: Added shadda to make the meaning stronger, emphasizing the sequence of events
This is a basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something

steps.m: to me (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding ىَّ (which is a form of ي with shadda) at the end changes it from just 'to' to specifically 'to me'. In this ayah, Allah is speaking about raising Jesus to Himself.
Added 'ma' at start and vowels to make it mean 'place of return' instead of just 'return'
Added كُمْ at the end to show this belongs to all of you. This word connects to إِلَىَّ (to Me) in the ayah, showing the return is to Allah.
This is a preposition showing location inside something
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he judged' to present tense 'I judge' by adding أ at start
Added فَ at beginning to show this action follows from what was mentioned before
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create the basic preposition form

steps.m: between (in فَأَحْكُمُ - so I will judge)
steps.n: Added fatha to show this word is connected to the verb فَأَحْكُمُ (so I will judge)

steps.m: between you all (in فَأَحْكُمُ - so I will judge)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this applies to all the people being addressed
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it connects to the verb تَخْتَلِفُونَ (you differ/disagree) to show what the disagreement is about.
Adding مَا turns this into a phrase meaning 'in what' or 'regarding what'. In this ayah, it introduces what people are disagreeing about.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to command form by dropping the middle letter و and changing vowels. Like saying 'exist!' instead of 'he existed'
Added تَ to show past tense for 'you'
Changed تَ to تُمْ to show multiple people being addressed
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. It doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: in it (referring to ما 'what' you differed about)
steps.n: Adds هِ meaning 'it' to show what is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to ما (what) that the people differed about.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show people disagreeing with each other
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by adding ت at start, and changed past to present tense
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This refers to disagreements about Jesus mentioned in the ayah.
This particle comes from a root meaning 'mother/origin' because it introduces the main or original topic being discussed
Adding ا at the end turns this into a special word that means 'as for' or 'regarding'

steps.m: and as for/so regarding (introduces the first part of ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning connects this to the previous thought and shows what comes next is a result or consequence. Here it introduces what will happen to those who disbelieve.
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like adding 'the' in English

steps.m: those who (describing the disbelievers mentioned in كَفَرُوا)
steps.n: Added some pronunciation marks and changed the shape of alif. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) which comes right after it, describing who did the disbelieving.

steps.m: to reject, to cover up, to be ungrateful for blessings
he rejected/disbelieved (subject of فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ - so I will punish them)
steps.n: Adding fatha vowels creates the basic past tense verb form. This word is part of the group that will be punished in the ayah.

steps.m: they rejected/disbelieved (subject of فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ - so I will punish them)
steps.n: Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural) - refers to a group of people who rejected faith

steps.m: to punish, to torture, to make suffer

steps.n: Adding shadda intensifies the meaning of punishment
Changes from doing the punishing to receiving the punishment
The فَ (fa) means 'so/then' and connects this to what happened before. Also changed from general 'to punish' to 'I will punish' - showing who is doing the action
Adding the attached pronoun هُمْ as the direct object to specify who is being punished
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun that describes the result of the action. This word is connected to أُعَذِّبُ (I will punish) in the ayah.
Adding ًا at the end shows this is what is being done to them - they will receive the punishment. It's the object of the verb أُعَذِّبُ (I will punish them).
Connected the letters with basic vowels to make a past-tense verb
Changed the word to describe something that has this quality very strongly. This word is describing the punishment (عَذَابًا) in this ayah.

steps.m: intensely (describing how the punishment will be)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to make it match with the word it describes (عَذَابًا/punishment)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or time. Here it connects 'punishment' to 'worldly life', showing where the punishment happens.
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under the ف to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'fee'
Changed the ي to ى which is just a writing style difference - it means the same thing
Root meaning 'near' was turned into a special pattern to make the word for 'worldly life' - the life that is closer to us now
Added ٱل to point to the specific worldly life mentioned in the Quran
The dot above the letter ن is combined with another ن using a shadda mark, making the 'n' sound stronger

steps.m: to come after or be delayed

steps.n: Adding vowels changes it from a root to an adjective meaning 'late'
Adding ة makes it feminine and changes meaning to 'the end/last part'
Added ال (the) and changed the spelling to show it means 'the afterlife' specifically rather than just 'last/end'
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'what' or 'not' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's negating the idea of helpers existing.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement about punishment. The combination means 'and not' here, showing they won't have any helpers.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the preposition ل
Added basic singular masculine pronoun ه with damma to show it's attached to the preposition
Changed singular هُ to plural هُم by adding م to show it refers to multiple people
Basic preposition showing where something starts, comes from, or is part of

The shadda is added because this word comes before another word that starts with ن. When مِن comes before a word starting with ن, the ن at the end of مِن combines with the beginning ن of the next word, shown by adding a shadda.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action
Added double dammah to make it indefinite (a helper)
Added ين to make the word plural (more than one helper)

steps.m: and as for (introduces the topic ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before. أَمَّا introduces a new topic (in this case 'those who'), while و connects it to the previous discussion.
This is the basic form of the word used to point to someone or something. Added kasra and ي to make it work with Arabic grammar.
Added ين at the end to make it plural (more than one) instead of singular (one)
Added ال at the beginning to point to specific people - in this case, those who believed (ءَامَنُوا۟)
Changed the writing style of ال to ٱ and added shadda on ذ to show it connects strongly with the ال before it. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added ءَا at the start and fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb. This word connects to الَّذِينَ (those who) in this ayah.
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This describes the group that الَّذِينَ (those who) refers to.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make past tense verb
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
Added و with fatha to connect this action with the previous word ءَامَنُوا (they believed)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who/what has this quality
Added feminine plural ending to refer to multiple good deeds rather than people
Added definite article and doubled the ص with shadda
Added kasra at end to show this word is the object of a preposition

steps.m: to fulfill, complete, or give in full

steps.n: Changed to past tense 'he fulfilled' and added shadda (double letter) to show doing something thoroughly/completely
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added فَ to show this happens as a result of what came before

steps.m: so he fulfills them completely (their rewards)
steps.n: Added هِمْ to show who receives the fulfillment. This connects to أُجُورَهُمْ (their rewards) in the ayah.
Root letters got vowels to make the noun meaning 'payment'
Changed from single reward (ajr) to plural rewards (ujoor) by changing the vowels and adding و
Added fatha to show this is the object receiving the action of 'He will give them in full' (فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ)
Added pronoun meaning 'their' at the end
This is the base word - the proper name of God in Arabic. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis on the ل.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject doing the action in يُحِبُّ (loves/likes).

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, and ٱ (hamzat al-wasl) which helps pronunciation when connecting words.
This is the basic negative particle that negates the verb that comes after it (يُحِبُّ/loves), making it mean 'does not love'
Added fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
The middle letter gets doubled with a shadda to show stronger meaning
Added يُ at the start to make it present tense (happening now). The middle vowel changed from a (َ) to i (ِ). Allah is doing the loving in this ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: one who acts unjustly (object of لَا يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: Forms active participle on pattern فاعل to indicate one who does the action. This word is the object (in accusative case) of the verb يحب (love), showing who Allah does not love.

steps.m: those who act unjustly (object of لَا يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: The ين ending makes it plural and shows it's receiving the action of not being loved

steps.m: the unjust ones (object of لَا يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: Adding ال (the) makes it specific - 'the wrongdoers' instead of just 'wrongdoers'
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's elongated when reading
Adding لِ changes 'this' to 'that', like pointing with your finger to something farther away
Adding كَ completes the word and makes it point to something specific that was mentioned before (in this case, referring to what was mentioned in previous verses)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. Changed vowels to show present tense. The و returns because it's now in the middle of the word.

steps.m: we recite it (referring to ذٰلِكَ - that)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show what is being recited. This refers back to ذٰلِكَ (that) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
When adding a pronoun after على, the ى changes to يْ. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon you (connected to نَتْلُوهُ - we recite it)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كَ meaning 'you' to show who is being acted upon. This word connects to نَتْلُوهُ (we recite it) to show what is being recited upon you.
This is the basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word ٱلْءَايٰتِ (the signs/verses). This is a helper vowel that makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear

steps.n: Makes the word plural by adding the feminine plural ending
Changes the word to genitive case, used when the word is possessed by something
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'

steps.m: the signs or verses (object of نَتْلُوهُ - we recite it)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific signs, changed alif to alif maksura (ٰ), and added kasra for grammar since it's the object being recited
Transforms verb into noun meaning 'act of remembering'
Adds emphasis through doubling of middle root letter
Adds definite article to make the noun definite
Adds conjunction at beginning to connect to previous word

steps.m: and the powerful remembrance/reminder (connected to مِنَ)
steps.n: Adds kasra at end to show connection to previous word مِنَ (from)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: wise one, one who is wise
steps.n: Forms an adjective meaning 'one characterized by wisdom'
Makes the word definite and shows it's describing Allah in this context
Added kasra at the end because this word follows مِنَ which requires the following word to end in kasra
Adding the shadda (ّ) strengthens the emphasis. This particle introduces and emphasizes the important statement that follows about Jesus's similarity to Adam.

steps.m: to be like something or compare things
steps.n: These three letters together carry the basic idea of comparing things or showing how they are similar
Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he compared'

steps.m: Jesus (the proper name of the prophet عليه السلام)
steps.n: This is a proper noun likely borrowed from Aramaic/Hebrew (Yeshua/Isa). While it has no Arabic root, its form in Arabic uses specific vowel patterns and endings.

steps.m: Jesus (the proper name of the prophet عليه السلام)
steps.n: The alif maksura (ى) at the end gets a small alif (ٰ) on top to show it's a special ending for proper nouns and to help with pronunciation
Adding kasra to first letter changes this from an action into a word about location
Adding fatha at the end shows this word connects to the word after it (اللَّهِ) to mean 'near/at Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added ال to make it specific and unique, doubled the ل with shadda, and added kasra at the end to show connection to what follows. This is the proper name for the One True God in Islam.

steps.m: to be like something or compare things

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it into a noun meaning 'example'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added كَ at the beginning to mean 'like' or 'similar to'. This word is comparing Jesus (عِيسَىٰ) to something.
The root letters were turned into the name Adam by adding vowels and stretching the first A sound

steps.m: Adam (as the object of خَلَقَهُ/He created him)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Adam is the object being created in this ayah

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing or existing materials

steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
The suffix هُۥ shows who was created (him/Jesus). This connects to the earlier part of the verse talking about عِيسَىٰ (Jesus).
This is a preposition showing that Adam was created from/out of dust. It connects the verb خَلَقَهُۥ (He created him) with تُرَابٍ (dust).
The pattern shows this is talking about the actual substance of dust/soil

steps.m: dust/soil (being used with مِن meaning 'from dust')
steps.n: Added tanween kasra because it follows مِن (from) in the ayah, showing what Adam was created from
Basic particle that shows sequence of events
Added damma and shadda to emphasize that this event happens distinctly after the previous one. This word connects قَالَ (he said) to what happened before it.
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
Added vowels to make it past tense, showing someone (he) did this action in the past
Changed the middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced
Added the pronoun ه with damma to mean 'him'
Added the small و mark to show proper pronunciation. This word connects to the command كُن (be!) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to come into being, to exist

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' form to command form by changing vowels and dropping last letter

steps.m: to come into being, to exist

steps.n: Changed root letters into a noun form by adding vowels (u sounds)
Adding يَ (ya) at the start changes it from just 'being' to 'he/it becomes' - like changing from a basic state to an action

steps.m: and so it becomes (immediate response to كُن)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happens immediately after Allah's command كُن (be!), emphasizing the immediacy of creation

steps.m: to be firm, established, or true

steps.n: Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning the essence of being true or real
Adding ال makes it refer to absolute truth rather than just any truth
The ُ ending shows this word is the subject of the statement, meaning 'This is the truth from your Lord'
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'the truth' (الْحَقُّ) with 'your Lord' (رَّبِّكَ), showing the truth's source.

steps.m: to nurture, care for, raise up

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ب shows this is someone who nurtures and cares for others completely
When a letter has two shadda marks, it makes the sound stronger to show extra importance
Adding kasra shows this word is connected to something after it

steps.m: your Lord (referring to the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Adding كَ shows this is your Lord, specifically referring to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in this ayah
This is a negative particle that makes something not happen. Here it tells تَكُن (you be) to not happen.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens because of something before it. Here it's because of 'the truth from your Lord' mentioned before.
Added damma and sukun to form basic noun pattern
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you'. The و is dropped because it's a weak letter in a command form. This is a negative command (don't be) because it comes after فَلَا (so do not) in the ayah.
Starting with the basic particle that means 'from'
The shadda on م shows that a ل has been absorbed into it. This happens because the word originally had ال (the) after it, but the ل merged with the م, making it stronger (shown by the shadda).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show who does the action, and ت between letters to show the doubting happens within oneself
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people who doubt

steps.m: the doubters (those who doubt the truth from your Lord)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to point to specific doubters mentioned in this verse
This is a question word that asks about people (like 'who' in English). When used like this, it can also mean 'whoever' - any person who does something.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before, like saying 'then' or 'so' in English
Changed from root letters to past tense verb with doubled middle letter to show intense arguing
Added alif after first letter to make it about the person doing the arguing
The alif was lengthened (آ) to show the arguing was done with a lot of back and forth
Added كَ at the end to show who was argued with
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.

steps.m: in it (referring to what was discussed in حَآجَّكَ)
steps.n: Added هِ to show what is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to the topic being disputed or argued about in حَآجَّكَ (whoever argues with you).

steps.m: from (connects بَعْدِ to what comes before)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. Here it connects 'after' (بَعْدِ) to the previous part of the sentence.

steps.m: from (connects بَعْدِ to what comes before)
steps.n: Added a small ن (called nun) at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters to form the base word
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create noun meaning 'after'
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'from after what came to you'

steps.m: what (connects to جَآءَكَ - came to you)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of the sentence. Here it links 'what' with 'came to you of knowledge', meaning 'what came to you of knowledge'
The middle letter ي changes to ا and combines with the fatha to make a long آ sound. This happens because ي is a 'weak' letter that often changes in Arabic verbs.
Added كَ at the end to show who was being arrived to/come to
Basic preposition indicating source, origin, or starting point
Added fatha at the end to connect with the next word العِلْمِ (the knowledge). This combination shows where something comes from.
Changed the verb into a noun by adding specific vowel patterns. This makes it mean the thing itself (knowledge) instead of the action (to know).
Adding ال makes it refer to specific knowledge - in this case, the knowledge mentioned earlier in the verse that 'came to you'

steps.m: of the knowledge (that came to you)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word comes after مِنَ (from) in the phrase 'from the knowledge'
The ْ (sukun) marks the end of the command form

steps.m: so say! (command in response to what came before)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this command is a response to the previous discussion mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added تَ at beginning to show doing something to yourself (like 'made himself high'), and ى at end shows it's a special form of the verb
Changed from 'he elevated himself' to 'come!' by adding وْا to make it a command for multiple people
Changed root letters into past tense 'he' form by adding the vowel marks
Changed to present tense form. The و at the end is from the root and shows up in present tense
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by replacing يَ with نَ at the start
The final و is dropped because this word connects to what comes after it (أَبْنَآءَنَا - our sons)
The root meaning 'to build' becomes 'sons' by adding أ at the start and ا after ب to make it plural - like turning 'son' into 'sons' but in a special Arabic way
Added نَا at the end to show these are 'our' sons. This word is being acted on by the verb نَدْعُ (we call) in this verse - we are calling our sons.
Added conjunction at beginning to connect to previous word أَبْنَآءَنَا (our sons)
Adds أ and ا to make plural form meaning 'sons' from root word meaning 'to build'
Added accusative case marker because this word is the object of the verb نَدْعُ (we call)
Added pronoun meaning 'your (plural)' - refers to the people being addressed in the argument
Changes root word into plural form meaning 'multiple women'
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
Adds possessive pronoun 'our' and elongates the alif for proper pronunciation
Root letters n-s-w become nisaa' - a special plural word meaning multiple women
The middle alif is lengthened to show it's a large group of women
Adds fatha to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
Adds كُمْ to show these women belong to the people being addressed

steps.m: and your women (as object of نَدْعُ/let us call)
steps.n: Adds و to connect this word to the previous items being called in the verse
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a singular noun
Changed from singular to plural by adding أ at the start and changing the middle vowel to u-sound (like in 'book')
Added و at beginning to connect with previous word
Added fatha to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: and ourselves (object of نَدْعُ - we call)
steps.n: Added نَا at end to show this belongs to "us". This word is one of the objects being called in the verb نَدْعُ (we call)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a singular noun
Changed to plural form for small quantities (3-10)
Added كُمْ to show possession by multiple people being addressed
Added وَ (and) to connect this word to the previous items in the list
Basic particle that shows one thing follows another
Added damma (ُ) and doubled the م with shadda (ّ) to make it a complete connecting word. This word connects to نَبْتَهِلْ (let us pray humbly) showing these actions happen in order.

steps.m: to pray earnestly or humbly beseech

steps.n: Changed from root letters to past tense verb by adding vowels
Added اِبْتَ (ibta) at the start to show doing the action to oneself with extra effort
Changed اِ to نَ to make it 'let us' (plural), and adjusted vowels to make it present tense instead of past
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added ن at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed vowels for present tense
The word changes its ending because it follows 'come' (تَعَالَوْا) - like saying 'come, let's make'
Added فَ at beginning to show this happens as a result of the previous action (come)
Adding fathas creates the past tense verb meaning 'he cursed'
Changed from a verb (he cursed) to a noun (curse) by making the middle letter silent
Added ة to make it a specific instance of cursing
Changed ending to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb نَجْعَل (we make/place)
Added shadda on first letter for emphasis after لَ
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows the ل is doubled.
Added ِ at the end to show this connects to the word لَّعْنَتَ (curse) before it, meaning 'curse of Allah'. Also changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, against, or directed at something else. In this ayah, it shows that Allah's curse would be upon/against the liars.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show it happened in the past
Changed the word to show who does the action instead of the action itself
Added ending to show we're talking about multiple people
Added 'the' at the beginning to point to specific people
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is the object of something
Adding the shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. This tells us that what follows (that this is the true story) is being strongly emphasized.
This is the basic attention-getting word that helps point to things
Adding ذا makes it specifically point to something masculine that's nearby

steps.m: this (pointing to the true story being told)
steps.n: Added the alif mark (ٰ) and fatha marks to show proper pronunciation. This word points to the story being discussed in this verse.
Added vowel marks to show this is an independent pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it'

steps.m: surely he/it is (emphasizes the predicate ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. This emphasizes that 'this' (هٰذَا) is indeed/surely 'the true narrative' (ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ)

steps.m: to tell a story in sequence

steps.n: Adding the first vowel mark to start forming the noun
Adding the second vowel mark to complete the noun pattern
Adding tanween (double damma) makes it 'a story' instead of just 'story'
Adding definite article makes it specific. This word is what هُوَ (it/this) refers to in this ayah.

steps.m: the story (subject of هُوَ and described by ٱلْحَقُّ)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this word is the subject being described as ٱلْحَقُّ (the truth) in this ayah
The damma (ُ) at the end shows this word is the subject or predicate in the sentence
Adding the definite article ال. This word is the predicate (what is being said about) the pronoun هُوَ in لَهُوَ (it is)
This is a negative particle used to negate what comes after it. In this ayah, it negates the existence of other gods.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects the statement about the true story of Jesus with the statement that there is no god except Allah.

steps.m: from (connecting إِلٰهٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: A preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects to إِلٰهٍ (god) to show there is no god from/among all possible gods except Allah.
Changed the root letters to make a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'. Like changing 'worship' into 'god'.
The small alif (ٰ) makes the 'aa' sound longer when reading

steps.m: any god/deity (after مِنْ meaning 'there is no')
steps.n: Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite ('any god') and follows مِنْ (from/of) in the phrase 'there is no deity except Allah'
Basic exception particle with hamza and shadda on lam
Added alif at the end to complete the particle. This word introduces an exception, showing that only Allah (ٱللَّهُ) fits the previous statement about divinity.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال at the beginning and the shadda on ل make it specific and definite. The ُ at the end shows this is the main subject being talked about.
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English.

steps.m: and indeed (connects to previous statement about Allah)
steps.n: Added 'and' at the beginning to connect this statement to the previous one. This connects the emphasis about Allah being the All-Mighty, All-Wise to the previous statement.
This is a unique word that refers specifically to Allah. The ال is part of the word itself, not just 'the'. The shadda emphasizes the L sound.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows Allah is being referred to or described in this sentence. The alif at the start is written differently (with hamzat wasl) because it connects with the previous word.
Added vowel marks to show this is an independent pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it'
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. This emphasizes that 'this' (هٰذَا) mentioned earlier in the ayah is definitely the true narrative.
Added vowels and ي to create a word showing a strong quality
Adding ال makes this a specific divine attribute of Allah
The ُ shows this is describing Allah directly in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an adjective describing someone who has deep wisdom
Makes the word definite by adding the definite article, showing this refers to Allah in this context

steps.m: the All-Wise (as one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Adds the nominative case ending because this word is part of Allah's name الْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (the All-Mighty, the All-Wise)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition
The فَ connects this condition to the previous word تَوَلَّوْا۟ (they turn away), showing what happens after they turn away

steps.m: to turn away or take charge

steps.n: Added vowels to make it past tense 'he' form
Doubles the middle letter to show intensity and drops the weak letter ي
Adding ت at start shows the person is doing the action to themselves

steps.m: they turned away intensively (from the truth after hearing it)
steps.n: Adds وْا at the end to show a group did this action. This word appears after فَإِن (and if) to show what happens if people reject the truth.
Added kasra (ِ) under the first letter and shadda (ّ) on the second letter to make the basic emphasis word

steps.m: then indeed (connects to تَوَلَّوْا۟ - they turn away)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this word to what happened before it. Here it connects to 'if they turn away' to mean 'then indeed'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging it with the word. The hamza is dropped and the first ل gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: Changed the first alif to a wasl form (ٱ) since it's connected to the previous word. Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being referred to in the sentence.
Added ي after first root letter and vowels to show someone who has this quality very strongly. This describes Allah in this ayah.
The ٌ (tanween) shows this is being used as a standalone name/description of Allah, rather than just describing another word

steps.m: All-Knowing (with emphasis on the completeness of knowledge)
steps.n: The ٌۢ adds extra emphasis to show how complete and perfect Allah's knowledge is

steps.m: to damage or make something bad

steps.n: Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show who does the action
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many people
Makes the noun definite

steps.m: with the people who damage things (connected to عَلِيمٌۢ - All-Knowing)
steps.n: Added بِ at the start to show connection. This word is connected to عَلِيمٌۢ (All-Knowing) in the ayah, showing that Allah knows about these people.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: say! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ - People of the Book)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) Dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) Adding damma ُ on first letter, 3) Adding sukun ْ at the end. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the People of the Book.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun referring to a group of people
Added a fatha mark at the end because this word is the object (something being acted upon) in the sentence
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to show we're calling out to these people. The long alif (ٰ) and hamza (ٓ) make the call stronger and help connect it to the next word.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together and show this happened in the past
This pattern changes a verb into a noun for the thing produced by that action. The word is possessed by أهل (people) in this ayah.
ٱلْ makes it specific (like 'the' in English), and the ِ shows it belongs to أهل (people)
Connected the letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding تَ shows something happening by itself rather than someone doing it to something else

steps.m: come! (all of you) (command to the People of the Book)
steps.n: Added وْا at the end to show this command is for multiple people. This word is addressed to the People of the Book (يٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. It's one of the primary prepositions in Arabic that isn't derived from a root.
Added kasra (ِ) under the إ to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'i-laa'
Added fatha (َ) above ل to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's a long 'aa' sound. In this ayah, it connects to the word كَلِمَةٍ (word/statement), showing what we're moving toward.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added feminine ending to create noun meaning 'word'

steps.m: a word (that comes after إِلَىٰ - to/towards)
steps.n: Added genitive case ending because it comes after the preposition إِلَىٰ (to/towards)
Root س-و-ي became سَوَآء by adding vowels and a long aa sound to make a word meaning 'equal' or 'same'
Added tanween kasra to show this word describes كَلِمَةٍ (word/statement) that comes before it in the ayah
Changes from verb 'to separate' into noun meaning 'between'

steps.m: between us (connected to وَبَيْنَكُمْ - and between you)
steps.n: Added نا to show this means 'between us'. This word pairs with وَبَيْنَكُمْ (and between you) in the ayah to show the relationship between two groups.
Adding vowels changes it from a root into a preposition meaning 'between'
The word 'between' gets 'kum' added at the end to mean 'between you all'
Coordinating conjunction و connects this phrase to previous elements
أَنْ is a particle that introduces what someone says, thinks, or wants to happen
Added لَا which means 'not' to negate what comes after

The ن from أَنْ combines with the ل from لَا to make a doubled ل (shown by ّ). This is just a pronunciation change, not a meaning change. This word introduces what we are being called to do in the ayah.
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' and changed vowels to show present tense
Changed last vowel to fatha to show this is after 'that' (أن)
This is an exceptive particle that introduces what is excluded from the previous statement. Here it connects to نَعْبُدَ (we worship) to show what we should exclusively worship.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being worshipped in نَعْبُدَ)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a special mark (shadda) to show strength, and a mark at the end (fatha) to show it receives the action 'we worship'
This is the basic negative particle that makes something 'not' happen. Here it negates the verb نُشْرِكَ (associate partners) that comes after it.
Adding و at the beginning connects this negative statement to what came before it in the ayah, showing these are related things that should not be done.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added نُ at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, changed vowels to show present tense
Basic preposition that shows how something is done or what is used

steps.m: with him/it (object of نُشْرِكَ - we associate)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هِ (him/it) to show what is being associated with. This connects to the verb نُشْرِكَ (we associate) in the ayah.
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters were given vowels (fatha and sukun) to make the common noun meaning 'thing'. This is what people are told not to associate with Allah in this ayah.
Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb نُشْرِكَ (associate). The meaning is 'we do not associate anything with Him'.
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'no'

steps.m: and not (connected to نَعْبُدَ - we worship)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative statement with the previous one. Both are part of what we agree not to do: not to worship other than Allah and not to associate partners with Him.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ت between first and second root letters and merged it with خ to show doing action to oneself. The initial أ changes to اِ in this form.

steps.m: that he takes/adopts (connected to أَلَّا in أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ)
steps.n: Changed to present tense with يَ prefix and added َ at end because it follows أَلَّا (that not). This word is part of a series of actions that are being negated.
Root letters given vowels to make the noun meaning 'some' or 'part'
Added damma to show this is doing the action in يَتَّخِذَ (takes/adopts)

steps.m: some of us (as subject of يَتَّخِذَ)
steps.n: Added نَا to show this means 'our' or 'us', making it 'some of us' who are doing the action of taking/adopting
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun

steps.m: some/part (as object of يَتَّخِذَ - takes as)
steps.n: Adds -an ending to show this word is receiving the action 'takes/adopts'

steps.m: to nurture, develop, or be master over

steps.n: Root ر-ب-ب became plural 'lords' by adding أَ at start and changing middle vowels to make أَفْعَال pattern
Added tanween fathatain to show this word is the object of the verb يَتَّخِذَ (take/adopt) in the ayah
Basic preposition showing something comes from or originates from something else
The letter م has a shadda because it comes after a word ending with ن - they combine together. It still means 'from' but here specifically means 'besides/instead of' Allah.
Adding damma changes it from a root to a usable word meaning 'below'
The kasra at the end shows this word connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) that comes right after it, making the phrase mean 'besides Allah'
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word دُونِ (besides/other than). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. Here it connects with تَوَلَّوْا (they turn away) to mean 'if they turn away'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous command (تَعَالَوْا - come). It connects the invitation with what to do if they refuse.

steps.m: to turn, follow, be close to

steps.n: Added shadda (doubled letter) to show intensity of action
Added تَ at beginning to show doing action to oneself
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Changed to قُولُوا by adding وا at the end to make it a command for a group (like saying 'all of you speak!')
Added فَ at the beginning to show this comes after something else

steps.m: to witness, observe, or testify to something

steps.n: Changed root letters into a command by adding vowel marks - telling someone to witness or testify
Added alif at the start to show it's telling someone to do something (command)
Added وا at the end to make it a command for multiple people
This is the basic emphatic particle that introduces a statement with certainty
Added 'ا' at the end to show this refers to 'we'

steps.m: that we are (connected to اشْهَدُوا - bear witness)
steps.n: Added 'بِ' at the beginning to connect it to the verb اشْهَدُوا (bear witness). This combination is commonly used after verbs of witnessing or testifying.

steps.m: to submit peacefully, to surrender oneself

steps.n: Added مُ at the beginning to show who does the action (like saying 'one who...')
Added ُونَ at the end to show we're talking about many people
Basic noun form of the root. This word is part of the phrase 'People of the Book' (أهل الكتاب) where it is possessed by 'the Book' (الكتاب).
Shows this word is owned by 'the Book' (الكتاب) in the phrase 'People of the Book'
Added يَا to directly address the people being spoken to
Changed يَا to يٰٓ for emphasis in Quranic style
Changed the verb into a noun by adding ِ on first letter and ا after second letter
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Changed the ending to show it belongs to something else (like 'of the book'), and changed the first A to a special A that connects smoothly when reading
Starting with the two separate particles: لِ (for/to) and ما (what)
The ما is shortened to مَ when combined with لِ to form the question word 'why'. This word is asking about the action تُحَآجُّونَ (do you argue) in this ayah.
Adding fathas creates the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he argued with someone (showing mutual debate)
steps.n: Added alif after first root letter to show two people debating each other
Changed to present tense by adding تُ at start, making it 'you argue' instead of 'he argued'
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is a preposition that shows something is inside, within, or about something else. Here it connects to the word إِبْرٰهِيمَ (Ibrahim) to show they are arguing about/concerning Ibrahim.
Added a small alif (ٓ) at the end to help with pronunciation when continuing to the next word. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a proper name of the prophet Abraham, borrowed from Hebrew. It has no Arabic root since it's a borrowed name.

steps.m: Abraham (as the object being discussed/argued about in تُحَآجُّونَ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Abraham is the object being discussed in the argument mentioned in the verb تُحَآجُّونَ (you argue/dispute)
This is a question word used to ask about non-living things or abstract concepts
The alif is written as آ to show it's pronounced longer. This is just a writing difference, not a meaning change.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to make it mean 'caused to descend/sent down' instead of just 'descended'
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it
Added ت to match with التَّوْرَىٰةُ (Torah) since it's feminine, and a connecting vowel to join with the next word
This is a borrowed word from Hebrew 'Torah' that was adapted into Arabic. The root meaning relates to light/guidance.
Added the feminine ending ة with an alif before it to make it a noun referring to the scripture
Added ال (the) to make it 'the Torah' instead of just 'Torah'
The ُ shows this word is the subject. The alif is written as ى with a small alif above it because it's at the end of the word.
This is a borrowed word from Greek 'evangelion' meaning good news, adapted into Arabic.
The 'ال' makes it refer to the specific holy book, not just any gospel or good news.
Added 'وَ' (and) at the beginning to connect it with the previous word (Torah). The 'ُ' at the end shows this is part of what was sent down after Ibrahim.
This is a basic exception particle that shows something is being excluded. It's not derived from a root but starts as a simple two-letter combination.

steps.m: except (excluding what comes after from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Adding the alif at the end helps connect this word to what follows it. In this ayah, it excludes what comes after (the time after Ibrahim) from what was mentioned before (the time of the Torah and Gospel).
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects with the word بَعْدِهِۦٓ (after him) to mean 'from after him'.
Added a small ن mark (called tanwin) after the final ن to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word.

steps.m: to be after or behind something

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fat-ha and sukoon) to the root letters to make the word mean 'after'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something and follows the word مِنۢ (from)
Added هِ to show this refers to Ibrahim (him)
Added elongation mark to هِ to emphasize 'him'
This particle turns a statement into a question, like adding a question mark in English
Added فَ which shows this follows from what came before - in this case, the previous discussion about Ibrahim and the Torah and Gospel

steps.m: then do you not? (connected to تَعْقِلُونَ)
steps.n: Added لا which makes this negative. This phrase questions why people don't use their reason (تَعْقِلُونَ) given what was just mentioned.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix for 'you' and adjusted vowel pattern

steps.m: you all use reason (addressing the People of the Book)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people are being addressed. In this ayah, it's addressing the People of the Book (أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ)
This is an attention word used to draw focus to what comes next, like saying 'hey!' or 'look!' in English. The alif is written as a long vowel mark.
The small alif (ٓ) is added to extend the sound for emphasis when calling attention
Added the word 'antum' (أنتم) which means 'you all' to specify who is being addressed. The hamza (أ) helps connect the two words smoothly.
This is the attention-getting part that starts many demonstrative pronouns. The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's shortened.
The small vertical line (called a madda) shows we should stretch the sound a bit when saying it.
Added ؤُلَآءِ which makes it point to multiple things or people that are near. This word is the subject (doer) of حٰجَجْتُمْ (you argued) in this ayah.

steps.m: to argue, debate, or present evidence

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show intensity of argument
Added تُ at the end to show past tense first person singular
Changed ending from تُ (I) to تُمْ (you all) to show many people did the action
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'concerning'

steps.m: in what (concerning which you have no knowledge)
steps.n: Added ما which makes this word ask 'in what?' This word is asking about what the people are arguing about, even though they don't have knowledge about it.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for you all (object of لَيْسَ - is not)
steps.n: Added كُم to show who the word 'for' applies to. This word is connected to لَيْسَ (is not) in this ayah, showing what is not for them.
Added kasra to show this connects to what comes after it
Added هِ to show what the preposition connects to. Here it refers back to عِلْمٌ (knowledge) mentioned in the ayah
Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation helper to show the vowel is long
Changed the verb (to know) into a noun (knowledge) by adding the vowels ِ and ْ
Added ٌ to show this is the main subject being talked about in this part of the sentence
Starting with the question word 'ma' (what), which is shortened from 'maa' in this context
Adding 'li' (for) changes 'what' to 'why' (literally: for what)
Adding 'fa' (so/then) connects this question to the following verb تُحَآجُّونَ (you argue), showing consequence
Added alif to show two people are involved in the argument
Added تُ to show people arguing back and forth with each other
The ُ at the end shows this is happening in the present
Changed to show back-and-forth arguing between two people (like a debate), with the long alif showing it's stretched out
Added ون at the end to show many people are doing the action
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it connects with the word عِلْمٌ (knowledge) to show what you have knowledge in/about.

steps.m: in what/concerning what (related to عِلْمٌ - knowledge)
steps.n: Adding مَا makes this ask about or refer to something specific. In this ayah, it's asking about what specific things they have knowledge of.
This makes it into the common negative word meaning 'is not'
Adds a fatha at the end to make it a complete verb meaning 'is not'
This is a basic preposition showing possession or purpose
Added كُم to show this is addressing multiple people. This word relates to عِلْمٌ (knowledge) in this ayah, showing 'knowledge for/to you all'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'

steps.m: with him/by it (referring to عِلْمٌ - knowledge)
steps.n: Added هِ which shows this refers back to 'knowledge' mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: with him/by it (referring to عِلْمٌ - knowledge)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation guide
Changed the verb into a noun by adding the vowels ِ and ْ to make it mean the thing itself (knowledge) instead of the action (knowing)
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is any knowledge, not a specific knowledge
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the doer of the action in يَعْلَمُ (He knows)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before, and added ٱ (hamzat al-wasl) which helps pronounce the ل
Added vowels (fatha-kasra) to make it mean 'he knew' in the past
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This shows Allah's continuous knowledge, as opposed to temporary knowledge.
This is a pronoun that means 'you' when talking to more than one person. In this ayah, it's the subject (doer) of the verb لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (you do not know)

steps.m: and you all (subject of لَا تَعْلَمُونَ)
steps.n: The وَ at the start means 'and', connecting this pronoun to the previous statement about Allah knowing
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you know) into its opposite meaning (you do not know). It's like adding 'not' in English to make something negative.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adds تَ at start to change from 'he knew' (past) to 'you know' (present), and changes vowels
The ون suffix changes the verb from singular 'you know' to plural 'you all know'
This is a negative particle that makes the sentence mean 'Ibrahim was not'. It works with the verb كَانَ (was) to say what Ibrahim was not.
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added fathas to make it past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning. This word tells us what Ibrahim was.
This is the basic form of the prophet's name, borrowed from Hebrew 'Avraham'. The long ā (ٰ) shows it's a foreign name adapted to Arabic pronunciation.

steps.m: Abraham (as the subject of كَانَ - was)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject (doer) of the verb كَانَ (was) in the phrase 'Abraham was not...'
Changed from a verb about following Jewish ways to a noun meaning 'Jews' by adding the vowel mark damma
Added يَ at start and ِيّ at end to make it mean 'Jewish' or 'relating to Jews'

steps.m: Jewish (as object being described in كَانَ إِبْرٰهِيمُ)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is describing Ibrahim in a negative statement (was not)
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
The ان ending makes it describe someone who does a lot of helping
Added يّ to show this person belongs to or follows the group of helpers. Historically, this word came to mean Christian because it referred to those who followed Christ's helpers/supporters.
Added ًا at the end to show this word is being acted on by كَانَ (was) in the phrase 'Ibrahim was not a Christian'
This is the basic word that shows contrast with what came before

steps.m: and but/however (contrasts with يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrast with the previous statement. This word contrasts with the earlier statement that Ibrahim was neither Jewish nor Christian.
Added fathas to make the basic past tense form. This word is related to يَكُونُ (he is) in the present tense.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a past tense verb. This is a common change with middle-weak verbs.

steps.m: to turn away from falsehood toward truth
one who turns completely to true faith
steps.n: Creates an intensive adjective describing someone who fully turns away from falsehood to embrace true faith. The ي shows this is a strong, permanent quality.

steps.m: as one purely devoted to true faith (describing إِبْرٰهِيمُ)
steps.n: Adds ًا at the end to show this describes Ibrahim - how he was purely devoted to the true faith
Changed root letters into noun by adding 'i' vowel in middle
The مُ prefix shows who does the action
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning

steps.m: being one who truly submits (to Allah)
steps.n: Adding tanween fatHa shows this describes Ibrahim's state
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects to the verb كان that comes after it.

steps.m: he was (past tense verb in مَا كَانَ)
steps.n: Added fathas to make it past tense. This word appears in مَا كَانَ (was not) which negates something in the past.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (the polytheists)

steps.m: to join or associate something with something else

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the action

steps.m: multiple people who join/associate partners (with Allah)
steps.n: Added ين to show we're talking about multiple people

steps.m: the polytheists (those who associate partners with Allah)

Added kasra (ِ) under the alif to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added shadda (ّ) on the ن to make the emphasis stronger. This word introduces and emphasizes the statement that follows about who is closest to Ibrahim.
Adding vowels to make it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he was close to'
Added أ at the beginning to show this is the most/closest form. The weak letter ي changes to ى at the end. This word connects to إِبْرٰهِيمَ (Ibrahim) in the ayah to show who people are closest to.

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be social' into noun meaning 'people'
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: of the people (possessed by أَوْلَى - closest/most worthy)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or is connected to أَوْلَى (closest/most worthy)
This is a proper name that doesn't come from a three-letter root. It's the name of Prophet Abraham in Arabic.
The letter ب with kasra (بِ) at the start shows connection or means 'with', 'by', or 'through'
The fatha (َ) at the end shows this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. Here, it shows who people are closest to.
Starting with the basic demonstrative particle that points to something specific
Added ال with shadda to make it a specific relative pronoun
Added ن at the end to make it plural
Added لَ at beginning to add emphasis, showing this is definitely/surely true
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added اِ at start and doubled the ت with ّ to show doing something with extra effort
Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this

Added the alif symbol (ٰ) above the ه to show the correct pronunciation

Added vowels to connect the root letters and form a basic noun

Changed the hamza to a shadda for easier pronunciation
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added ال to make it specific and shadda to strengthen the meaning
Added ين at the end to make it plural, showing we're talking about multiple people
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

Added plural masculine past tense suffix وا to show multiple people did this action. This word describes الَّذِينَ (those who) in this ayah.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence
Added وَ at beginning to connect this to the previous statement, and added ٱ (wasl form of ال) after it

Added alif at start to show causing someone else to do the action

This is the base word مَا which can mean 'what' when asking questions or 'that which' when making statements. In this ayah, it's used with the verb يَشْعُرُونَ (they perceive) to say 'they do not perceive'.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This word connects to يَشْعُرُونَ (they perceive) to form the meaning 'and they do not perceive'.

Changes to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.n: Adds ون to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the people from the People of the Book who wish to lead others astray.

steps.m: to be settled, to belong to a place/people

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning the group that belongs together

Added يا with a long alif (ٰ) and a small alif (ٓ) to call out to the people. The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it (ٱلْكِتٰبِ). Together they mean 'O People of the Book!'

Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
Adding the genitive case marker ِ to show possession or after preposition
This is the basic preposition meaning 'for'
When لِ combines with مَا (what), the ا is dropped to form لِمَ meaning 'why'. This word is asking about the action تَكْفُرُونَ (you all disbelieve) in this ayah.

Adding يَ at the start (not ت) changes it from past to present tense
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing ya- with ta- at the start

Adding long alif at start makes the word stronger/more emphatic

Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by Allah (اللَّهِ) that follows it

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال at the beginning and the shadda on ل make it specific and definite. The kasra at the end shows it belongs to or connects with something else in the sentence.
This is a pronoun that means 'you' when talking to multiple people. It's the subject (doer) of the verb تَشْهَدُونَ (you witness) in this ayah.
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ
Added ون to show this refers to multiple people

steps.m: to live somewhere as a family/community
people (especially of a place or group)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the people who belong to or live in a place

steps.m: O people! (addressing ٱلْكِتٰبِ - the People of the Book)

Changed from action (write) to the result of that action (book). This pattern is commonly used to show what results from an action.
Added ٱلْ to make it specific (the book), and ِ at the end shows it belongs to أَهْل (people) in the phrase أَهْل ٱلْكِتَابِ (People of the Book)
Starting with the two separate parts: لِ (for) and مَا (what)
The مَا is shortened to مَ in questions. This word is questioning the action of mixing truth with falsehood mentioned right after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix showing 'you' are doing the action

steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to mixing/confusing the truth (الْحَقَّ) with falsehood (بِٱلْبٰطِلِ)
The doubled letter ق shows this is absolute or complete truth
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific truth. This truth is what is being hidden (تَكْتُمُونَ means 'you hide')
The َ at the end shows this truth is being acted upon - it's what is being hidden
Changes from verb 'to be false' to noun meaning 'something false'
Added ال to make it specific
Added بِ at beginning to show 'with' and ِ at end to connect it to the sentence
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to present tense form by adding تَ at start and changing vowels to show ongoing action
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action (addressing a group)
Added conjunction و to connect with previous phrase

This is a pronoun that refers to multiple people being spoken to. It's the subject (doer) of the verb تَعْلَمُونَ (you all know) in this ayah.
The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this clause to the previous statement about concealing the truth
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
The middle letter و changes to ا when we add the past tense pattern. This is a common change with middle-weak verbs.
Adding ت at the end changes 'he' to 'she'. This word is describing what طَّآئِفَةٌ (a group) did.

Adding tanween dammah makes the noun indefinite. This word is the subject (doer) of قَالَت (she said) in this ayah.
