Added ال to specify these particular people. The ن gets a shadda because the ل from ال combines with it
Added لِ at start to show this is for/to the people. This word comes before وَهُدًى (guidance) in the ayah, showing who the guidance is for
The word means 'for the people' - two ل letters combine into one with shadda, and the ending gets a kasra (small diagonal line) because it connects to the word 'guidance'

steps.m: to guide or show the right way

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to guide' into noun meaning 'an act of guidance'
The ى at the end makes it indefinite (a guidance)
Added وَ (and) to connect this word with بَيَانٌ (explanation) from the previous part of the ayah

steps.m: to advise with wisdom and care

steps.n: Changed the word from an action into a thing by adding مَ at the start and ة at the end. The وْ after first letter and ِ on ع show this is something that results from the action.
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is 'a' lesson rather than 'the' lesson

steps.m: and an advice/lesson (connected to بَيَانٌ - clear explanation)
steps.n: Added وَ at the start to connect this word with the previous word بَيَانٌ (clear explanation), showing they go together
The root word 'protect' becomes 'one who protects themselves' by adding مُ at start and changing و to ت with a shadda
Regular sound masculine plural ending

steps.m: for those who protect themselves (from evil)
steps.n: Adds 'for/to' meaning to the word

steps.m: for those who protect themselves (from evil)
steps.n: The doubled ل shows emphasis and connection to the next word
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with the previous phrase. This word connects to تَهِنُوا (become weak) to say 'and do not become weak'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make the past tense form
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و from original word drops in present tense.
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start
Added وا at the end to make it plural (addressing multiple people). The alif at the end (۟) is just a writing mark showing it's a command not to do something.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَحْزَنُوا۟ (be sad) into 'do not be sad'
The و connects this with the previous phrase وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ (and do not be weak), making a list of things not to do
Added vowels to connect the root letters. The pattern shows a feeling or emotion.
ت at start shows either 'you' (male) or 'she' is doing the action
Added وا at the end to show this is addressing multiple people
The small و mark shows this is for multiple people
This is the basic pronoun used when talking to multiple people (2 or more). In this ayah, it's addressing the believers.
Added و at the beginning to connect it with the previous phrase 'do not grieve'. This connection shows these ideas are related.
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the next word الْأَعْلَوْنَ (the highest/superior ones).
This root is about being high up or superior
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added أ at beginning to show comparison. The ا at the end changes to ى in this form.
Added ون at the end to make it plural. The ى changes back to و before this ending.

steps.m: the highest ones (you are the highest ones)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite. This word describes أَنتُمُ (you) in the ayah.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'if you are believers' to the previous statement about being superior/uppermost.

steps.m: to exist or be in a state
steps.n: This root is about existing or being in a state
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The weak letter و drops when followed by a sukun, and the first vowel changes to match what follows

steps.m: you all were (condition for being ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ - uppermost)
steps.n: Added تُم to show past tense 'you all'. This word is part of a conditional statement - 'if you were' - relating to being uppermost/superior mentioned just before.
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'someone who believes/trusts' instead of the action itself
Added ين to show we're talking about many people
The doubled م adds emphasis to show strong/firm belief

steps.m: if (introduces condition about يَمْسَسْكُمْ - touching you)
steps.n: This is a basic word that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it sets up 'if injury touches you' as the condition.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present/future tense. Changed vowels to sukun because it comes after إِن (if). In this ayah, it means if harm touches you.

steps.m: touches/will touch you all (if إِن happens)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show the touch happens to 'you all'. This connects to إِن (if) at the start of the ayah, meaning 'if harm touches you all'.
This is the basic meaning showing what happens when something wounds or injures something else
Changes from being a verb (to wound) into a noun (a wound) by adding specific vowel marks
Added tanween to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a wound' instead of 'the wound'). This wound is what's doing the afflicting in the phrase 'if a wound touches you'
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. قَدْ is used to emphasize that something definitely happened.
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what follows. Here it connects to the verb مَسَّ (touched), showing a sequence of events.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. This word is related to يَمْسَسْكُمْ (touches you) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
The middle س combines with shadda. Here it shows how an injury (قَرْحٌ) affected or touched a group of people.

steps.m: to stand firmly or rise up

steps.n: Changes verb root into a noun meaning a group of people
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific group of people rather than any group
The fatha at the end shows this group is being affected by the action مَسَّ (touched/afflicted) in this ayah
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning 'wound'. This word is what's doing the touching/afflicting in this part of the verse.
Added double-damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in an indefinite form (like saying 'a wound' instead of 'the wound')

steps.m: to be similar or equal to

steps.n: Adding the kasra (ِ) changes this from a verb root into a noun meaning 'similar'
Adding the sukoon (ْ) on the middle letter completes this common noun pattern
The damma (ُ) at the end shows this noun is the subject
Added هُ with extension mark ۥ to mean 'it/him', referring to the wound/harm (قَرْحٌ) mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: that/those (feminine, pointing to الْأَيَّامُ - the days)
steps.n: This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something far away. In this ayah, it's pointing to 'the days' (الْأَيَّامُ) that come right after it.

steps.m: and that/those (feminine, pointing to الْأَيَّامُ - the days)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this phrase with the previous statement about wounds affecting both sides
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Changed to plural by adding أ at start and doubling ي with a shadda to mean multiple days
Added ال to make it specific days
Added ُ to show this is the subject of the verb نُدَاوِلُهَا (We alternate them)

steps.m: to take turns or exchange back and forth
he exchanged back and forth with someone
steps.n: Added an alif after first letter and fatha marks to show two parties taking turns with each other
Added نُ at start to show 'we' are doing it, and changed the middle vowel to kasra to show it's happening now

steps.m: we alternate it (referring to الأيام/the days)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show what is being alternated (the days mentioned before)
Adds 'it' as the direct object pronoun. The pronoun ها refers back to الأيام (the days) and is the object receiving the action of alternating.

steps.m: to be separate/distinct from something else

steps.n: Forms a word meaning 'between' that shows the space or relationship separating things. In this ayah, it's specifically talking about alternating days between people.

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others

steps.n: The initial hamza (أ) is dropped in common usage
Added ال (the) at the beginning and kasra at the end because it comes after بَيْنَ (between). The ل merges into the ن creating a shadda.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make it past tense
Added يَ at the start to change from past tense (he knew) to present tense (he knows), and the middle letter got quieter (sukun)
Added لِ to show purpose ('in order to/so that')

steps.m: and so that he knows/learns (connected to نُدَاوِلُهَا - We alternate them)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this clause with the previous statement about alternating days between people
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Starting with the basic demonstrative particle that points to something
Changed the ending to make it a relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ين to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال to make it specific - 'those who' instead of just 'who'
Added vowel marks and changed ال to ٱ for proper pronunciation
Adding alif makes it mean 'to actively put trust in something'
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to وَلِيَعْلَمَ (and so that He [Allah] may know) in this ayah.
Connected letters and added basic past tense vowels
The doubled ت shows this is done with more effort or intensity than regular 'taking'
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ (kasra). Still shows taking something with effort.

steps.m: and he takes for himself (connected to لِيَعْلَمَ - so that He may know)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this word to the previous phrase. This word is connected to لِيَعْلَمَ (so that He may know) in the ayah.
Starting with the basic preposition مِن which shows something coming from or out of something else
Added the suffix كُمْ which means 'you all'. In this ayah, this word is connected to وَلِيَعْلَمَ (and that He may know), showing who Allah wants to know about.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added ي to show someone who does the action as their role
Changes to plural form for multiple witnesses/martyrs
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity/god
Added a double L sound (shadda) to change from 'any god' to 'Allah' - the One True God
Added fatha on the shadda for pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show Allah is the subject (doer) of the verb لَا يُحِبُّ (does not love)

steps.m: and Allah (as the subject of لَا يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, like saying 'and'
Basic negative particle that makes something 'not' happen. Here it negates the verb يُحِبُّ (loves) to mean 'does not love'
Added fatha (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Adding fatha vowels creates the basic past tense verb form
The double letter shows intensity or emphasis in the action
Adding يُ at the start and changing the vowels shows this happens in the present/future. The ِ (kasra) on the middle letter is part of this present tense pattern.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes from verb to active participle meaning 'one who wrongs'. This word is the object (in accusative case) of the verb يحب (love), showing who Allah does not love.

steps.m: people who act unfairly (who Allah does not love)
steps.n: Makes the word plural masculine. This word is the object (in accusative case) of the verb يحب (loves), showing who Allah does not love.

steps.m: the people who act unfairly (who Allah does not love)
steps.n: Added 'the' at the beginning and emphasized the first letter with shadda

steps.m: to purify or test by removing faults

steps.n: Added shadda to show intensive purification, like really cleaning something well
Added يُ at the start to show it's happening now or in the future
Added لِ to show why something is happening

steps.m: and so that he purifies thoroughly
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this with what came before
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Changed to the unique name of The One True God by adding ٱل, doubling the ل with shadda, and adding ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in this verse
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it specific and point to particular people. The word is followed by ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) which tells us who these people are.
Basic vowels (fat-ha) added to make it 'he' past tense
Changed the first letter to hamza and added alif, which makes the meaning stronger and more specific to faith/belief
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: to destroy completely, to wipe out

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added يَ at start and sukun on ح to show this is happening now or in future

steps.m: and he destroys completely (connected to يُمَحِّصَ)
steps.n: Added و at start to connect this action with the previous word يُمَحِّصَ (purify)

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changes from an action to describing someone who does that action

steps.m: rejecters/those who cover up truth (acted upon by يَمْحَقَ)
steps.n: Makes it plural and shows it's being acted upon by the verb يَمْحَقَ (destroy/eliminate)
Makes it specific which rejecters we're talking about and adds a final vowel to show it's being acted upon
This is a question particle that connects to what comes after it (حَسِبْتُمْ - 'do you think'). It's like saying 'or' when starting a question to challenge what someone thinks.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'I' by adding تُ at the end
Changed from 'I' to 'you all' by adding م with sukun. This word connects to أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ (that you would enter) to mean 'Did you all think that you would enter...'
This is a connecting word that links حَسِبْتُمْ (you thought) to what was thought (تَدْخُلُوا۟ - you would enter). It's like saying 'You thought that you would enter.'

steps.m: to enter, go inside, or become part of something

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it 'he entered' in the past
Changed to present tense with ت prefix. The vowel marks also change to show ongoing action.
Added وا at the end to show the verb is addressing multiple people
Added small high waw (۟) as a pronunciation guide
Adding vowel marks makes it a basic past tense verb
Added ة to make it a noun referring to a specific place that is covered/protected
Added ال to specify this particular garden, referring to Paradise
Added َ at the end to show this is the object being entered in the phrase 'that you will enter Paradise'

This is a basic particle that shows something hasn't happened yet but is expected to happen. In this ayah, it connects to يَعْلَمِ (know) to say Allah has not yet known/distinguished those who strive.

steps.m: and not yet (connects to يَعْلَمِ - knows/distinguishes)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This shows this is part of a continuing thought.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense form
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, changed middle vowel to sukun, and added ُ at end for indicative mood
Changed the ending vowel to kasra (ِ) to show jussive mood - used when the verb follows certain particles
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'

Changed to the unique name of the One True God by adding ال with a special spelling (ٱ instead of أ), doubling the ل with shadda, and adding ُ to show this is the subject doing the action of knowing (يَعْلَمِ) in this verse
This is the basic form used to point to someone or something being described
Added ٱلْ at the beginning with a shadda to make it specific. The ل gets a shadda when connected to ذِي.
Added ينَ at the end to make it plural. This word points to the people who did جٰهَدُوا۟ (strived/struggled) in this ayah.

steps.m: to strive hard, exert maximum effort

steps.n: Added vowels (a-a-a) to make it mean 'he strived hard' in the past
Added alif to show mutual action - meaning someone strives with or against others
Added وا to show multiple people (3 or more men) did the action
The alif changed from short (َا) to long (ٰ), but the meaning stays the same - multiple people striving
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone

steps.m: from you all (referring to the believers being tested)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' ending to show this applies to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the believers who are being tested.

steps.m: to know, understand, or be aware of

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense, with sukun on ع to make pronunciation easier. The fatha at the end shows this is subjunctive mood because it comes after و (and) in this context.
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this verb to what came before it in the verse
Added an alif after ص to show this is a person who does the action of being patient
Adding ين at the end makes it plural - changes from 'one patient person' to 'many patient people'

steps.m: the ones who endure difficulties calmly (object of يَعْلَمِ)
steps.n: Added ال at beginning to make it definite. The shadda on ص is because of merging ل with ص. This word is the object (receiver) of يَعْلَمِ (He knows) in this ayah.
This is a common particle in Arabic that adds emphasis to past actions
Adding لَ makes the emphasis even stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed'

steps.m: and indeed definitely (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: وَ connects this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
This is one of the most common roots in Arabic
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The middle و drops out and is replaced with sukun, while the first vowel changes to damma - this is a common pattern with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)
Added تُ at the end to mean 'I' - changes 'be/exist' to 'I was'
Changed تُ to تُمْ to show this is addressing multiple people (you all). This word is the subject of تَمَنَّوْنَ (you all wished/desired) in this ayah.
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added shadda to show the wishing is done with intensity
Added تَ at beginning to show doing the action to/for oneself

steps.m: you all are wishing intensely (for الْمَوْتَ - death)
steps.n: Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action in the present tense. This verb refers to how the believers were wishing for martyrdom before actually facing it.
Adding vowel marks to show this is a noun meaning 'death'

steps.m: the death (object of تَمَنَّوْنَ - you wish for)
steps.n: Added ال to point to a specific death being talked about

steps.m: the death (as the thing being wished for)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this is the thing being wished for in the sentence
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'you wished' (تَمَنَّوْنَ) with 'before' (قَبْلِ) to show when the wishing happened.
Added fatha and sukun to make a noun meaning 'before' that can be used to tell when something happens
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after مِن (from/before)

steps.m: that (connects to تَلْقَوْهُ - you would meet it)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces what someone wants, expects, or plans to happen. Here it connects the wanting of death (تَمَنَّوْنَ ٱلْمَوْتَ) with actually meeting it (تَلْقَوْهُ).
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'he met'
Added تَ prefix to show 'you' and changed vowels for present tense. The ي at the end becomes ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changed ى to وْ to show that many people are doing the action

steps.m: you all meet him/it (object of تَمَنَّوْنَ - you wish/desire)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being met. This word is being acted on by the verb تَمَنَّوْنَ (you wish/desire) in the ayah.
This is an emphasis particle that makes the meaning stronger. Here it emphasizes that they definitely saw death.
The فَ shows this is a result of what was mentioned before - they wished to see death, and so indeed they saw it

steps.m: to see or observe with one's eyes

steps.n: Changed from basic root 'to see' into past tense 'he saw' by adding vowels and writing with ى at the end
The ى changes back to ي when more letters are added after it
Adding تُ shows that 'I' did the action
Adding و at the end means the action was done to 'him'

steps.m: you all saw it (referring to الْمَوْتَ/death)
steps.n: Added هُ to show what was seen (referring back to death/الْمَوْتَ mentioned earlier in the verse)
This is a pronoun for talking to a group of people. It's the subject (doer) of the verb تَنظُرُونَ (you are looking) in this ayah.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ (then you saw it), showing these actions happened in sequence

steps.m: to look at or watch something

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Added تَ at beginning to change from 'he' to 'you', and changed final vowel
Added ونَ at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
This is a basic negative particle that makes the statement 'Muhammad is not...' in this ayah
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it
Adding مُ at the start and َ on the middle letter shows who receives the action of praising
Adding shadda (gemination) on middle letter intensifies meaning to indicate repeated/frequent praise
The double dammah (tanween) at the end shows this is Muhammad's name standing alone in a sentence
This is a basic exception word that limits what comes before it. It's not derived from a root since it's a fundamental particle in Arabic.
Adding shadda emphasizes the limiting nature of the word
Adding alif at the end helps connect this word to what follows it. In this ayah, it connects to رَسُولٌ (messenger) to say 'Muhammad is nothing except a messenger'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb (to send) to a noun (messenger) by adding و
Added double damma to show this is the subject of إِلَّا (except/only) in the phrase 'Muhammad is only a messenger'
This is a particle that emphasizes the verb خَلَتْ (passed away) that comes after it. It shows that this passing away definitely happened in the past.

steps.m: to pass away or go before

steps.n: The root letters خ-ل-و became a past tense verb خَلَا meaning 'he passed away' - the و changed to ا at the end

steps.m: she passed away (referring to رُسُلٌ - messengers)
steps.n: Added feminine marker ت with sukun to show this refers to رُسُلٌ (messengers) which is treated as feminine in Arabic even though it refers to male messengers
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects with قَبْلِهِ (before him) to show 'from before him'
Added vowel marks to make the word mean 'before' as a direction or time
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'before him'. The ِ before هِ connects it smoothly. This whole phrase comes after مِن (from).

steps.m: to send or be sent as a messenger

steps.n: The و makes it mean someone who does this action a lot
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple messengers
Added 'the' (ال) at the start, which made the ر double in sound

steps.m: the messengers (subject of قَدْ خَلَتْ - have passed away)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this word is the subject of the verb قَدْ خَلَتْ (have passed away)
This is the basic conditional particle that shows something depends on something else
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added أَ at the start to turn it into a question, like adding 'is it?' in English. The ي is just to help say it smoothly
Root letters م-و-ت became مَاتَ by changing middle و to ا in past tense 'he died'
Added emphasis with shadda. This word is connected to انقَلَبْتُمْ (you all turned back) in the ayah, showing what would make them turn back.

steps.m: or (connecting مَّاتَ 'he died' with قُتِلَ 'he was killed')
steps.n: This is a simple conjunction particle that connects two possible scenarios being discussed about Prophet Muhammad ﷺ - whether he died naturally or was killed in battle
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he was killed (in response to مَّاتَ - he died)
steps.n: Changed vowels to show that someone else did the action to the subject. This word appears in a question about whether Muhammad (SAW) was killed or died naturally (مَّاتَ).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The prefix ان shows the action happens to oneself. This is a verb, not a noun, so it doesn't have case endings.
Adding تُ at the end changes 'he' to 'I' in past tense

steps.m: you all turned back (in the past)
steps.n: Changed تُ (meaning 'I') to تُمْ (meaning 'you all') at the end to show many people did the action
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or against something else. In this ayah, it connects to أَعْقٰبِكُمْ (your heels) to show the action of turning back upon one's heels.
Added an alif that shows the long 'aa' sound at the end of the word. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word. This is just a pronunciation mark and doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to make the basic singular noun for 'heel'
Plural form meaning physical heels, follows regular broken plural pattern
Adds kasra at the end to show the word is the object of a preposition
Added suffix to show possession by multiple people
Combines و (and) with من (who/whoever)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Adding يَ at start changes it from past to present tense
Adding ن changes it from doing the action to the action happening to oneself
This is a preposition that shows being higher than or on top of something. It doesn't come from a root - it's one of Arabic's basic building blocks.
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition that shows one thing being on or over another thing.
The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is connected to أَعْقٰبِكُمْ (your heels) in the ayah, meaning 'turning back on your heels'.

steps.m: to follow behind or trace back

steps.n: Basic noun form showing something concrete (heel) or abstract (consequence)
Added ي to make it mean 'my heels' (not 'two heels')
Added sukun before adding possession suffix
Added هِ to show these are his heels. This word is the object of عَلَىٰ (upon) in this ayah.
This particle shows that what follows is a result or consequence of what came before
Added لَن which is used to strongly negate something in the future. In this ayah, it's saying that turning back will never harm Allah.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make 'he harmed', doubled the ر to show strong/intense harm

steps.m: he will harm (after فَلَن meaning 'will never')
steps.n: Added يَ at start to show present/future tense and changed first vowel to ُ. This verb comes after فَلَن (will never) which makes it mean 'will never harm'
Added shadda on ل to show emphasis and wasl alif at beginning for pronunciation
Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being harmed in the phrase 'will not harm Allah'
The root letters were turned into a noun by adding marks - a fatha (short 'a' sound) on ش and a sukoon (no vowel) on ي. The hamza at the end completes the word meaning 'thing'.
Added ًا at the end to show this is what's being affected by the action (in this case, what won't harm Allah)

steps.m: to give what is deserved (reward or payback)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he will give what is deserved
steps.n: Added سَ to show this will happen in the future

steps.m: and he will give what is deserved (to الشَّاكِرِينَ - the grateful ones)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this with what came before. The reward will be given to the grateful ones mentioned next in the ayah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he thanked' to 'one who thanks' by adding alif after first letter and kasra under second letter - this pattern shows who does the action

steps.m: thankful people (acted upon by يَجْزِى/will reward)
steps.n: Changed from 'one who thanks' to 'thankful people' (plural) - these are the people receiving Allah's reward

steps.m: the thankful people (acted upon by يَجْزِى/will reward)
steps.n: Added 'ال' (the) at the start and a fatha mark at the end to show these are the specific thankful people being rewarded
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. This word can mean either 'what' (for questions) or 'not' (for negation). In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's used with كان to say something 'is not'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This combination (وما) with كان means 'and it is not' in this ayah.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.

steps.m: he was (describes what happened to نَفْسٍ)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic to make words easier to pronounce. In this ayah, it describes the state of نَفْسٍ (a soul).
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun form that means one soul or self

steps.m: for a soul (that will die according to تَمُوتَ)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for', showing this soul is the thing that the action happens to
This is a particle that connects to the verb تَمُوتَ (dies/would die) to show what is not possible for any soul. It helps express the meaning 'that it would die' in this context.
Added damma to create basic verb form
Added تَ at beginning to show present tense for 'she' or 'you'
Added َ at end to show this is in the subjunctive mood (after 'that')
This is a basic word that shows an exception. It tells us that the dying mentioned in تَمُوتَ can only happen under certain conditions.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the result of giving permission
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is done through or by means of permission. This word connects to اللَّهِ (Allah) which comes after it.
Added ِ at the end because it's connected to the word after it (اللَّهِ)
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a proper noun for The One True God
Added kasra at the end to show possession, doubled the ل with shadda, and changed initial alif to wasl form. This word is possessed by إِذْنِ (permission) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he wrote' (verb) to 'book/writing' (noun) by using a special pattern. The middle letter changes from 'a' to 'i' and adds a long 'aa' sound in the middle.
The word changed to show it's being acted upon - like saying 'a book' vs 'wrote a book'. The long alif (ٰ) and tanween marks (ًا) at the end show this change.

steps.m: to delay or set a time limit

steps.n: Doubled the middle letter to show doing something with extra strength or purpose
Added مُ at the start to show this is the thing being delayed (like changing 'delayed' to 'delayed thing')
Added ًا at the end to show this word is being used as the object in the sentence
This is a word used to talk about any person in general. Here it refers to the person who desires something.
The وَ at the start connects this part to what came before it, showing another possibility or option
Root letters connected with vowels (i) and (u) to make a noun meaning 'wanting'
The weak letter و is dropped because it has a sukun and comes after a kasra

steps.m: he wants/seeks (subject of نُؤْتِهِ - we will give him)
steps.n: Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense and male subject. This word is connected to نُؤْتِهِ (we will give him) in the ayah - if he wants/seeks something, we will give it to him.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make it mean 'reward'. This word is what is being desired in the phrase 'desires reward'.
Final fatha marks this noun as being in the accusative case, which is appropriate since it's the object of يُرِدْ (to want/desire)
Added damma and sukun to make it into a noun meaning the quality of being close
Root means 'close' - added ي and ا to make it mean 'the closest/nearest (life)', like our present world
Added ال to make it specific. The ل combines with د to make it doubled (shadda)

steps.m: to give, to bring, to come

steps.n: Changed to passive form by adding damma (ُ) and kasra (ِ) marks to show something is being given/brought
Added نُ at the start to show 'we' are doing the action, and the hamza moved after the ن with a sukun

steps.m: we give him (referring to ثَوَابَ - reward)
steps.n: Added هِ suffix meaning 'him/it' with ۦ elongation mark. The 'him' refers to the reward (ثَوَابَ) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing something comes from somewhere else

steps.m: from it (referring to الدُّنْيَا - this worldly life)
steps.n: Added هَا which refers back to الدُّنْيَا (this worldly life) mentioned earlier in the verse. This shows we're talking about getting reward from this worldly life specifically.
This is a basic relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it's the subject (doer) of the verb يُرِدْ (wants/desires).
The prefix و adds the meaning of 'and', connecting this clause to the previous statement about worldly rewards
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense. The middle letter و changed to ي, making it easier to say. Common pattern for this type of verb.

steps.m: he wants/seeks (subject of نُؤْتِهِ - we will give him)
steps.n: The word is shortened because it comes after 'whoever' (مَن) in an if-then statement. Like saying 'If someone wants...' in English. The middle ي is dropped and the end gets a stop mark.
Added vowel marks (fat-ha) to the root letters to create a noun meaning reward/return
Added alif after second root letter to make it a noun meaning the result of returning/rewarding. This word is what is being wanted/desired in the phrase 'whoever wants the reward'.
The آ at start shows this means 'more/most after' = 'last'
Adding ة makes this a feminine noun meaning 'the end/last part' which came to mean 'the afterlife'

steps.m: the Hereafter (reward of which is mentioned in ثَوَابَ)
steps.n: Added 'the' at beginning and 'of' marker at end. This word is connected to ثَوَابَ (reward) in the ayah, showing what kind of reward is meant.
Connected the root letters together

steps.m: we give (in response to يُرِدْ - whoever wants)
steps.n: Added نُ at start to show 'we' and changed vowels to show present tense. This is the response part of 'whoever wants... we will give'

steps.m: we give him (referring to whoever wants the reward)
steps.n: Added هِۦ at the end to show who receives the giving - refers back to 'whoever wants' from earlier in the verse
Added sukun to show the word ends here before joining with next part
Added هَا at the end to show this is happening to/from something feminine. In this ayah, it refers back to الدُّنْيَا (the worldly life) mentioned earlier.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by adding ن at start and changing vowels

steps.m: we will give what is deserved
steps.n: Future tense marker added before the imperfect verb

steps.m: and we will give what is deserved (to الشَّاكِرِينَ - the thankful ones)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the start to connect this promise of reward to what came before it
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: one who thanks (acted on by نَجْزِى/we will reward)
steps.n: Changes the verb into a person who does the action. This describes someone who will receive the reward mentioned in نَجْزِى (we will reward).
Regular masculine plural form in accusative case. This word is the object (the ones receiving the action) of the verb نجزي which means 'We will reward'.
Adding definite article to make the noun definite
This is a special word used to express amazement at a large quantity, similar to saying 'how many!' in English
Added ن at the end with kasra under the shadda to complete the word form
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of
Added shadda because it comes after the word وَكَأَيِّن. When مِن comes after certain words, it gets a shadda for easier pronunciation.
Added ي between second and third letters to show this describes someone who does this action as their special role
Adding shadda makes the last letter stronger to show this is someone's permanent role/title
The double kasra shows this means 'of a prophet'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after first letter to show this is about fighting with others, not just one-sided killing. This word relates to رِبِّيُّونَ (devoted worshippers) who fought alongside prophets.
This is a preposition showing accompaniment - being together with someone/something

steps.m: with him (referring to the prophet نَّبِىٍّ mentioned before)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show this means 'with him', referring back to the prophet mentioned earlier in the verse
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Doubling the ب makes it stronger - shows complete lordship/mastery
Changing the vowel to kasra (ِ) creates a variant form with similar meaning
Added ي (yaa) at the end to mean 'my Lord' - the shadda makes it stronger and more personal

steps.m: many people devoted to the Lord
steps.n: Added ون to make it plural, showing many people
Added vowels to make it mean 'it became many' in the past
Changed from a verb (action) to a word that describes something as 'many' by changing the middle vowel
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this word describes 'devout believers'
This is a negative particle that negates the verb وَهَنُوا (they lost heart) in this ayah, showing they did not lose heart
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows as a result - they fought alongside their prophet, and then as a result, they did not lose heart
Added vowel marks to make 'he became weak' - like adding 'a' sounds between letters
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action. This word comes after ما (not) in the ayah, so it means 'they did not weaken'.
Starting with the preposition لِ which shows reason or purpose
Added ما which means 'what' to show 'because of what'
Added the elongated alif (آ) which is just a spelling variation of regular alif
Added basic past tense vowels. The middle root letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter).
Added أَ at the beginning to make the verb causative - showing that something caused the hitting/striking/affecting to happen

steps.m: it afflicted/struck them (in response to ما وَهَنُوا - they did not lose heart)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who was affected. This word explains what happened to them in response to the previous phrase 'they did not lose heart'.
This is a basic word that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it shows being 'in' the path/way of Allah.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added ي to make the word a noun referring to a concrete path
Added kasra at the end to show this is Allah's path. This connects it to the next word اللَّهِ (Allah).
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added kasra at the end to show possession, shadda on ل, and changed initial alif. This shows it belongs to the word سَبِيلِ (path) in the phrase 'path of Allah'
Basic negative particle that negates the verb that follows it (ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ meaning 'they yielded')
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative statement with the previous one, showing these actions happened together - they neither weakened nor yielded
The vowel pattern َُ shows this means 'becoming weak' rather than 'making weak'
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action
This is a negative particle that makes the verb ضَعُفُوا۟ (they became weak) into 'they did not become weak'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
This root conveys the basic idea of submission or yielding
Changed from the root letters to past tense by adding the vowel marks 'a' sounds
Added است at the beginning to show actively seeking this state
Changed to show multiple people doing the action
This is a unique proper name that doesn't come from a standard root. It's the personal name of God in Islam.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يُحِبُّ - loves)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is doing the action in this sentence - Allah is the one who loves.

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of يُحِبُّ - loves)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and'
This root represents the concept of love and affection
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
The middle letter gets doubled to show stronger meaning
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to show this is happening now. This word tells us what Allah does - He loves.
This root represents the concept of patience and endurance
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show this describes someone who has this quality
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and shadda on ص because it comes after ال

steps.m: the patient ones (object of يُحِبُّ - loves)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to make it plural and show it's being acted upon. In this ayah, Allah is the one who loves (يُحِبُّ) these patient ones.
This is a special word that negates the verb كَانَ (was) in this ayah, making it mean 'was not'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it mean 'he was' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak middle letter in past tense. This word introduces what their statement was.
Changed from verb 'to say' into noun 'a saying' by adding vowel marks (fatha and sukun)
Added هُمْ at the end to show possession. This word is the object of كَانَ (was) in this ayah.
Added shadda (ّ) to emphasize the meaning. This makes the word stronger.
Added long alif (آ) at the end to connect this word with what follows it. In this ayah, it connects to 'أَن قَالُوا۟' (that they said).
This is a particle that introduces what someone said or thought. Here it connects 'their saying' (قَوْلَهُمْ) to the actual words they said.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)
The suffix وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine plural)'
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who does complete and perfect caring

steps.m: our Lord (being called upon in قَالُوا)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show we are talking about our Lord. This is what is being said after قَالُوا (they said), like in English: 'they said, "our Lord!"'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make the command form - sukun on first letter, kasra on second letter, and sukun at the end

steps.m: forgive! (command to Allah in رَبَّنَا's prayer)
steps.n: Added alif at beginning to help pronunciation of command form. This word is part of the believers' prayer to their Lord (رَبَّنَا)
Added fatha to the letter ل to show it's connected to what comes after it
Added نا which means 'us' to show who the action is for. This word is connected to اغْفِرْ (forgive) in this ayah, showing who is being forgiven.

steps.m: to make a mistake or commit a wrong

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make the basic noun form for a single sin or mistake
Changed the vowels and added و to make it plural, showing many sins
Added نَا at the end to show these are our sins. This word is what is being forgiven in the phrase 'forgive our sins'
Changes the verb into a noun that names the action itself. Like how 'transgress' becomes 'transgression' in English.
Adds و (and) to connect this to the previous word ذُنُوبَنَا (our sins) - both are things being asked to be forgiven

Adds نَا (our) to show this transgression belongs to the speakers. This word is being acted upon by the verb اغْفِرْ (forgive) - they are asking Allah to forgive both their sins (ذُنُوبَنَا) and their transgression (إِسْرَافَنَا).
This is a preposition that shows location or relation. Here it connects to أَمْرِنَا (our affair/matter) to show 'in our affair'.
The ى with ٓ (alif) is just a spelling variation of ي that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed from basic root meaning 'command' into noun meaning 'matter' by adding vowels
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Added نَا to show this belongs to "us"
Adding shadda makes the meaning more intensive - showing a strong action of making something firm

steps.m: and make firm! (command in a sequence of prayers)
steps.n: Added و (meaning 'and') at start + changed to a command form asking Allah to make something firm
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels
Changes the word from an action (stepping) to the body part used for that action (feet). The أ at the beginning and ا after د create a broken plural pattern common for body parts.
Adding نَا shows possession (our feet). The fatha (َ) before نَا shows this word is being acted upon - it's what is being made firm in the phrase ثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا (make our feet firm).
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he helped' to 'help!' by adding u- at start and changing middle vowel - makes it a command
Added نَا (na) at the end to mean 'us' - changes from 'help!' to 'help us!'

steps.m: and help us! (connected to previous requests in the prayer)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this request to the previous prayers in the verse
This is a preposition that shows position or relation. In this ayah, it connects with the word كٰفِرِينَ to show who the believers are asking for victory against.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'people' - those who stand together as a group

steps.m: the people (being fought against by المؤمنين)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ِ at the end because it comes after على (upon/against) in the ayah

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Changes verb into noun meaning 'one who does this action'
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple people

steps.m: the ones who reject/cover up truth
steps.n: Makes the word specific by adding 'the'

steps.m: the ones who reject/cover up truth (object of عَلَى)
steps.n: Changes short 'a' sound to long 'aa' sound (كٰ) and adds a small 'a' sound at the end to show it's being acted upon
Changed from basic root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels and writing ى at the end
Added فَ at the beginning to show this action follows something before it
Changed to Form IV by lengthening the first vowel, which changes meaning from 'come' to 'give'. Allah (اللَّهُ) is doing this action.
Added هُمُ to show who received the giving (them). The ىٰ shows this is a long vowel.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added ال with special spelling, doubled the ل with shadda, and added ُ to show this is the subject of يُحِبُّ (loves) in this ayah
Connected the letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to create a noun meaning the result of returning/repaying - in this case a reward. This word is what Allah gave (آتى) to them.
Changed the root into a feminine noun meaning 'nearest/closest' which refers to this life (as opposed to the afterlife)

steps.m: the worldly life (contrasts with ٱلْءَاخِرَةِ 'the afterlife' in this ayah)
steps.n: Added 'the' to make it specific and doubled the د letter with shadda because it comes after ال
Added ُ and ْ to make it mean the quality itself instead of the action
Added َ at the end to show this is receiving the action of 'gave them' (فَـَٔاتَىٰهُمُ)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
This form turns the action of returning into a noun meaning something given in return (reward). This word is being possessed by الْءَاخِرَةِ (the Hereafter) in this ayah.
Adds -i ending to show this reward belongs to/is connected to something else

steps.m: to come after, to be delayed

steps.n: Changed the root into an active participle meaning 'last' by adding alif and kasra
Added feminine ending to create specific noun for 'the hereafter'
Added definite article to specify this is THE hereafter, contrasting with 'this world' mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added kasra to show this describes the reward (ثَوَابِ) mentioned in the ayah
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action (loving) in this sentence

steps.m: And Allah (subject of يُحِبُّ - He loves)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense
When two identical letters come together, they combine with a shadda
Added يُ at beginning to show present tense, and changed vowels to show ongoing action. This word describes what Allah does in this ayah.

steps.m: to do good, to be excellent

steps.n: Added short vowels (fatha-damma-fatha) to make it mean 'he was good'
Adding مُ at the start and changing vowels creates a word for someone who does the action
Adding ين makes it plural (more than one person doing good) and shows it's being acted upon by another word

steps.m: the ones who do good (object of يُحِبُّ - loves)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it definite and َ shows it's the object being acted upon
The root letters أ-ي-ي combine with a fatha and shadda to make أَيّ, which asks 'which' or 'who'
Added يَا at the beginning to make it a call for attention
Added the 'u' sound (damma) to show we are calling out to someone directly
Added ها to make it suitable for addressing someone in formal speech
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who'. We add the kasra (ِ) to help with pronunciation.
Adding ال at the beginning makes it point to specific people or things, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين at the end makes it talk about many people instead of just one person. In this ayah, it refers to the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا۟).
The alif at the beginning is written slightly differently (with a waṣla) because it connects with the word before it when reading
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to change meaning from just feeling secure to actively having faith
Added plural masculine past tense ending to show multiple people did the action
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition - it means 'if you do something, then something else will happen.' In this ayah, it introduces 'if you obey...' (تُطِيعُوا)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Added تُ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense. The و changes to ي in present tense.

steps.m: you (all) obey willingly (command to ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show this is addressing multiple people. This is a command to 'those who believe' mentioned earlier in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which'
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ن at the end makes it plural, changing it from 'the one who' to 'those who'. This word describes the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا۟) in this ayah.

steps.m: to cover up, reject, or deny truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb meaning 'he rejected'
Added وا with a small alif (۟) on top to show a group of people did this action

steps.m: to return something back to where it was

steps.n: Added يَ at start and combined the double د into دّ with a shadda to show present/future tense
Added و to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they will return you all (referring to the believers being warned not to let the disbelievers turn them back from their faith)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being returned - connects to context where believers are being warned about disbelievers trying to turn them away from faith
This is a basic preposition showing something is physically or metaphorically on top of something else. It doesn't come from a root.
Added ى (alif maksura) at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it. Here it connects to أَعْقٰبِكُمْ (your heels) to say 'upon your heels'.
Added ٰ (small alif) above the ى and ٓ (long vowel mark) to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to follow behind or leave footprints

steps.n: Changes from verb root to a concrete noun meaning the part of foot that follows
Changes singular 'heel' to plural form by adding alif at start and lengthening middle
Changes to 'heels' with a different spelling and ending because it comes after the word 'upon'
Adds second person masculine plural possessive pronoun. The word comes after على meaning 'upon your heels' in the context of turning back or retreating.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Added اِنْ at the beginning to show the action happens by itself or to oneself (like flipping over on your own)
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end, with ِ in middle
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ
Added وا to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: so you all turn yourselves over
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of something else
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes verb into noun describing someone who does the action

steps.m: losers (as object of verb يَرُدُّوكُمْ - they return you)
steps.n: Makes the word plural and shows it's being acted upon in the sentence. This word describes the state you will end up in if you obey the disbelievers.
This is a basic particle that introduces a statement that corrects or contradicts what was mentioned before
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because it connects to the next word اللَّهُ (Allah)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite and the shadda emphasizes the L sound. The ُ shows this is the subject of the sentence.

steps.m: to be close to and protect

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a past tense verb meaning 'to protect' or 'be close to'
Changed from a verb (to protect) into a noun (protector) by adding مَ at start and changing the ending

steps.m: your protector (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show it's your protector. The alif with bar (ىٰ) helps show proper pronunciation.
Added diacritics to show the correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun 'he'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This word is the subject of خَيْرُ (best) in this ayah.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the comparative/superlative form
The ُ at the end shows this word is the predicate (completing the meaning) after هُوَ (He). Together they mean 'He is best'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to active participle form meaning 'one who helps'
Added masculine plural ending ين to show multiple helpers
Added definite article ال and fatha on final ن. This word follows خَيْرُ (best of) in the ayah, showing Allah is the best of helpers.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense
Changed to present/future tense by adding يُ prefix and changing vowels
Changed subject to 'we' by replacing يُ with نُ
Added سَ prefix to emphasize future tense. This word acts on قُلُوبِ (hearts) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing location or container. Here it shows where Allah will cast fear - into the hearts.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how it's pronounced
Changed the final ي to ى which is common at the end of words

steps.m: to turn/change (like a heart beating and turning blood)

steps.n: Changed from action to the thing that does the action (the heart that turns/pumps blood)
Changed the vowels and added و to show many hearts
Added kasra at the end because this word follows فِى (in) and shows these are the hearts of those who disbelieved
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to people or things
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'who'
Added final vowel mark and shadda for proper pronunciation. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in the ayah, describing who did the action.

steps.m: to reject, cover up, or deny truth

steps.n: Adding fathas connects the letters and shows this happened in the past
Adding وا shows many people did this action. This word describes who will receive fear in their hearts from the verb سَنُلْقِى (We will cast)
Root meaning 'terrify' becomes the noun 'terror' by adding vowel marks (damma and sukun)
Added the definite article ال which causes the first letter to get a shadda

steps.m: the terror (object of سَنُلْقِى - we will cast)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being cast into the hearts of the disbelievers
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' - it introduces a clause that describes something
Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'what' to 'because of what' or 'due to what'
The alif is written long (آ) at the end of words for clarity in reading, but doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form

steps.m: to make associate/to cause to share
steps.n: Added hamza with fatha (أَ) at beginning and made middle letter sukun to create Form IV pattern, which makes the action causative or transitive
Changed final vowel to damma and added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word relates to اللَّهِ (Allah) in the ayah as the one with whom they associated partners.
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It's a unique word that doesn't come from a typical root.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly
Added بِ at start to mean 'by/with' and ِ at end to connect it to the verb أَشْرَكُوا۟ (they associated partners). This shows what they wrongly associated partners with - Allah.
This is a relative pronoun that connects what comes before it to what comes after it. Here it connects بِ (with/by) to أَشْرَكُوا (they associated), meaning 'because of that which they associated'.
This is the basic form of the word - a particle used to negate verbs
Adding the vowel marks shows this word negates the verb يُنَزِّلْ (send down) that comes after it and changes its meaning to the past. It's like saying 'did not send down'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter ز shows that the action happens multiple times or with extra force

steps.m: he sends down repeatedly (in a negative context with لَمْ)
steps.n: Added يُ at start to show present tense, and sukun at end because it comes after لَمْ (which makes verbs negative)
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added pronoun ه with kasra to show this means 'him/it'

steps.m: with/by him/it (referring to what they associated with اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation guide. This word refers back to what they wrongly associated with Allah.

steps.m: to have control or rule over something

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'power' by adding the vowel marks ُ (u) and ْ (sukoon)
Added ٰن to intensify the meaning

steps.m: any authority/clear proof (that He did not send down)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence - specifically referring to the authority that Allah did not send down
Added مَ at the start to mean 'place of' and ى at the end because the last root letter is weak
Added conjunction وَ (and) at the beginning of the word

steps.m: and their shelter (connected to النَّارُ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added هُمُ at the end to show it belongs to them. This word is connected to النَّارُ (the Fire) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Changed the root into a noun for fire, which comes from the concept of light/shining
Added emphasis to show the intensity of the fire
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the intense fire (subject of مَأْوَىٰهُمُ - their refuge)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence, specifically what their refuge is
Changes from basic root to past tense verb expressing strong disapproval

steps.m: and how evil! (connects to مَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ - their refuge is the Fire)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this statement of disapproval with the previous statement about their refuge being the Fire

steps.m: to stay or settle in a place

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to show where something happens, and ى at the end to make it a noun. The ْ (sukun) on و shows it's a place noun. This word describes where the wrongdoers will stay.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
This pattern shows someone who does the action - like adding '-er' in English (teach → teacher)
The ين ending makes this plural, showing there are many wrongdoers
Adding ال makes it specific, like 'the' in English. The ظ gets a shadda because it comes after ال
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of this emphasis word
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis, like saying 'surely' or 'definitely'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it

Added elongation mark to show this is a formal/Quranic spelling

Added تَ at start and ا after first letter to show people doing action to each other

The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

Added ال to make it definite (the) and ِ at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah
Added fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb. The weak letter ي changes to ى at the end.

Added ون at the end to show that many people are doing the action
